generated_requirements.text: The system shall support queue-based automatic observing as the default operational mode, with manual interactive operation available only when explicitly enabled by operations staff.
generated_requirements.rationale: BR-OM-01 and Objective 1 mandate queue-based observing as the standard mode to maximise scientific productivity.

generated_requirements.text: The Sequencer shall automatically execute preprogrammed observing sequences from the queue, exploiting all possible concurrencies in telescope and instrument operation to minimise elapsed time per observation.
generated_requirements.rationale: Objective 1 identifies the Sequencer as the primary mechanism for achieving operational concurrency and maximising science throughput.

generated_requirements.text: The scheduling decision-support tool shall present operations staff with a ranked list of candidate observing programmes optimised against current site conditions, instrument configuration, Cassegrain rotator cable-wrap position, and observatory-directorate priority rules, but shall not autonomously select or execute a programme without human confirmation.
generated_requirements.rationale: BR-SQ-02 and assumption A-09 require human-confirmed scheduling decisions; domain expertise adds cable-wrap as a mandatory scheduling constraint.

generated_requirements.text: The observing queue shall be resortable at any time during operations based on configurable, table-driven rules supplied by the observatory directorate, without requiring software recompilation.
generated_requirements.rationale: BR-SQ-01 and RC-01 require dynamic resorting governed by directorate rules that must be configurable without code changes.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall support instrument and telescope mode switching at least twice per night, with all overhead for reconfiguration accounted for within defined overhead budget limits.
generated_requirements.rationale: BR-SQ-03 and Objective 1 require flexible scheduling with at least two mode switches per night.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall define an Observing Block (OB) as the atomic unit of observation; a running OB shall not be interrupted mid-exposure except under fatal safety conditions, and any preempted OB shall be logged with partial-credit accounting and returned to the queue in a restartable state.
generated_requirements.rationale: Domain expertise identifies OB atomicity and preemption rollback as critical for queue integrity and science credit accounting.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall provide a service observing workflow that allows Gemini staff to access a client astronomer's submitted observing programme, review programme-specific notes and special instructions, adjust execution parameters within permitted bounds, execute the programme on behalf of the absent astronomer, and log all actions taken against the programme record.
generated_requirements.rationale: Section 3.5 identifies service observing as a primary stakeholder need with no existing software support; BR-SQ-04 requires all observations to be queueable as preprogrammed sequences.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall provide an integrated communication facility enabling service observers and operations staff to exchange text or voice-linked messages with remote client astronomers during an active observing session.
generated_requirements.rationale: Section 3.5 and Section 4.2 explicitly require the ability for service observers to communicate with remote client astronomers during programme execution.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall provide a visually oriented observing programme environment in which astronomers can create, edit, validate, and submit automated observing programmes, including simulation of programme execution against the virtual telescope before submission.
generated_requirements.rationale: Section 4.2 and Section 6.1 scope in a rich programme environment with full simulator validation to eliminate programme errors as a source of telescope time loss.

generated_requirements.text: Every hardware subsystem shall provide a software simulation module that responds correctly to all commands directed at that subsystem; simulation modules shall operate within the standard VxWorks/EPICS/VME environment and shall not require any application-specific hardware.
generated_requirements.rationale: TC-09 mandates simulation modules for every hardware subsystem to enable virtual telescope operation and programme validation.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall implement a layered safety architecture enforced in the following strict precedence order: software range/validity checks → software motion limits → soft limit switches → software watchdogs → hardware watchdogs → hard limit switches → hardware interlocks → mechanical hard stops. No software or hardware action shall be capable of bypassing a hardware interlock.
generated_requirements.rationale: BR-SF-02 and BR-SF-03 mandate the layered safety architecture with an absolute prohibition on hardware interlock bypass.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall automatically bring itself to a defined safe state upon detection of any safety-relevant condition without requiring human intervention, and shall log the triggering condition, the safe-state transition taken, and the time of onset.
generated_requirements.rationale: BR-SF-04 requires autonomous safe-state transition; BR-FE-04 requires all fault notifications to identify origin and be electronically logged.

generated_requirements.text: Every IOC process and every supervisory software process shall emit a heartbeat signal at a configurable period not exceeding 1 second; the watchdog monitor shall declare a process failed and initiate the defined recovery procedure if no heartbeat is received within a configurable silence interval not exceeding 5 seconds.
generated_requirements.rationale: Domain expertise identifies heartbeat/watchdog timeout specification as essential to achieving the 5-minute recovery target stated in Objective 2.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall classify all errors into exactly three categories — Fatal, Serious, and Warning — and shall take the following automated actions: Fatal: transition to safe backup state and require human restart; Serious: halt the affected operation and require human intervention to resume; Warning: log the condition and continue operation.
generated_requirements.rationale: BR-FE-01 defines the three-tier error classification and mandates specific automated responses for each tier.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall distinguish alarms from errors; automatically monitored alarms shall clear automatically when the triggering condition resolves, while alarms requiring human acknowledgement shall remain active and visible to all operators until explicitly acknowledged by an authorised user.
generated_requirements.rationale: BR-FE-02 requires a formal alarm/error distinction and defines the two alarm lifecycle models.

generated_requirements.text: All fault and alarm notifications shall identify the originating subsystem, the specific error code, and a human-readable description; notifications shall be electronically loggable and shall support at least three verbosity levels (summary, standard, and diagnostic) selectable per user session.
generated_requirements.rationale: BR-FE-04 requires origin identification, electronic logging, and multiple verbosity levels for all fault notifications.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall automatically retry commands that fail due to timeout or no-response conditions, using a configurable retry count and back-off interval, before escalating to the next error severity level.
generated_requirements.rationale: BR-FE-05 requires automatic command retries for common timeout conditions to avoid unnecessary error escalation.

generated_requirements.text: Failure of any single subsystem or IOC shall be isolated such that it does not cascade to affect the operation of other subsystems, communication links, or the observing queue.
generated_requirements.rationale: BR-FE-03 mandates failure isolation to prevent cascade failures across the distributed system.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall maintain a minimum of three privilege levels — Observing Astronomer, Operations Staff, and Software Development/Maintenance Staff — with privileges determined at login via a single logon operation and enforced throughout the session for all commands, status queries, and configuration changes.
generated_requirements.rationale: BR-AC-01 mandates three privilege levels with login-time determination and session-wide enforcement.

generated_requirements.text: Observing astronomers shall have no privilege to issue direct control commands to the telescope mount or telescope subsystems; they shall have read-only access to all subsystem status and operational tables, and direct control access to instruments only.
generated_requirements.rationale: BR-AC-02, BR-AC-03, and BR-AC-04 define the specific command and access boundaries for the Observing Astronomer privilege level.

generated_requirements.text: Access to the Test operational level shall be restricted to Software Development/Maintenance Staff; at test level the system shall permit installation of new software packages, execution of any low-level test command, and update of all non-protected parameter values, while other parts of the system may concurrently operate at maintenance or observing level.
generated_requirements.rationale: BR-OM-05 and BR-AC-06 specify that test-level access must be extremely limited and define the capabilities available at that level.

generated_requirements.text: The Access Mode Allocation system shall exclusively assign critical resources that support only a restricted number of simultaneous uses; the allocation algorithm shall be provably deadlock-free.
generated_requirements.rationale: BR-AC-09 requires exclusive allocation of restricted resources through a deadlock-free mechanism.

generated_requirements.text: Remote access to system features shall be restricted to explicitly authorised sites (e.g. Hale Pohaku, Hilo, Cerro Tololo, partner institution nodes); the security system shall enforce site-based and user-based access controls configurable without software recompilation to reflect current partnership agreements and applicable export control obligations.
generated_requirements.rationale: BR-AC-08, RC-03, and domain expertise on export control (US EAR/ITAR) require configurable site-based access enforcement.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall support simultaneous operation of up to six active control nodes and two monitoring nodes (one local, one remote) as the nominal configuration, and shall remain operational with up to ten active nodes under peak load conditions.
generated_requirements.rationale: Objective 3 specifies the nominal six-node and peak ten-node multi-site operational requirements.

generated_requirements.text: Remote operations nodes shall provide full functional equivalence to on-site operations; any authorised user shall be able to access any part of the system from any node via a single logon, subject to privilege controls, with only performance degradation (not functional loss) permitted under WAN bandwidth constraints.
generated_requirements.rationale: Objective 3 and Section 4.2 require full functional equivalence for remote operations over constrained WAN links.

generated_requirements.text: Read-only monitoring users shall be able to observe all telescope and instrument status information from any network location without affecting the performance of ongoing observations; no status request shall cause a delay in control activities or produce a locking condition, even if the corresponding equipment is unavailable or faulty.
generated_requirements.rationale: BR-MO-01, BR-MO-02, and BR-MO-03 mandate non-blocking, any-point on-demand status queries that never degrade control performance.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall ingest meteorological data from the site weather station (wind speed, wind direction, temperature, humidity, barometric pressure, seeing estimate) and make this data available centrally to all users and to the scheduling decision-support tool in real time.
generated_requirements.rationale: BR-MO-04 mandates central availability of meteorological data; the scheduling tool requires current site conditions to rank observing programmes.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall provide software interfaces to site monitoring video cameras, autoguider cameras, cloud monitors, and seeing monitors, and shall distribute video frames at up to 0.5-second cadence using lossy compression (JPEG/MPEG) for rough monitoring; high-quality image transmission shall use lossless compression and complete within 20 seconds.
generated_requirements.rationale: Section 6.1 scopes in video and sky-monitoring interfaces; BR-DM-05 specifies the compression and timing rules for video versus science image transmission.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall provide computer access to on-line star catalogues enabling automatic selection of guide stars and standard stars for any target position, with selection criteria (magnitude range, colour, proximity) configurable by operations staff.
generated_requirements.rationale: Section 6.1 explicitly scopes in star catalogue access for automatic guide star and standard star selection.

generated_requirements.text: The autoguider control loop shall acquire a guide star within a configurable timeout, maintain closed-loop guiding corrections at a bandwidth sufficient to correct atmospheric tip-tilt at the required frequency, and implement a defined handoff protocol with the active optics system to prevent the two control loops from issuing conflicting mirror corrections.
generated_requirements.rationale: Domain expertise identifies autoguider closed-loop requirements and the active optics handoff protocol as critical, routinely omitted requirements for 8-metre class telescopes.

generated_requirements.text: The active optics system shall read out the wavefront sensor and compute mirror actuator corrections on a cadence of 30–120 seconds; the maximum latency between a wavefront measurement and the corresponding actuator command shall not exceed 5 seconds; mirror corrections shall be inhibited during detector integrations if vibration coupling to the science detector is detected.
generated_requirements.rationale: Domain expertise specifies the active optics correction cadence and vibration-coupling inhibit requirement for 8-metre primary mirrors.

generated_requirements.text: The telescope motion planning layer shall enforce hardware-derived maximum slew rates, maximum acceleration profiles, and minimum deceleration distances for the telescope mount; these limits shall be treated as hard constraints not configurable by any user privilege level.
generated_requirements.rationale: Domain expertise identifies mount drive hardware limits as hard constraints that must be enforced in the motion planning layer to prevent mechanical damage.

generated_requirements.text: The telescope control software shall support coordinate transformations between apparent place, mean place (FK5 and ICRS), observed place, galactic, and horizon coordinate systems, applying corrections for precession, nutation, stellar aberration, and atmospheric refraction, with a transformation accuracy of better than 0.1 arcseconds.
generated_requirements.rationale: Domain expertise identifies coordinate system transformation requirements as routinely omitted by non-astronomers but essential for correct telescope pointing and data header population.

generated_requirements.text: The telescope pointing model shall support a parameterised correction model with at least 20 terms; the model shall be loadable and updatable at runtime without interrupting ongoing observations; blind-pointing accuracy after model application shall be better than 2 arcseconds RMS on-sky.
generated_requirements.rationale: Domain expertise identifies pointing model parameterisation and runtime update capability as standard requirements for 8-metre class telescope control systems.

generated_requirements.text: The instrument field derotator control loop shall track the required field position angle in sidereal, non-sidereal, and fixed-position-angle modes; the control loop update period shall not exceed 100 milliseconds; the maximum allowed field rotation error shall not exceed 0.1 arcseconds during a science exposure.
generated_requirements.rationale: Domain expertise identifies instrument rotator/derotator field-angle tracking as a distinct control loop requirement with specific accuracy and update-rate constraints.

generated_requirements.text: The scheduling and sequencing software shall track the Cassegrain rotator cable-wrap position for each candidate observing programme and shall require a wrap-unwind procedure to be scheduled and accounted for in the overhead budget when the rotator approaches its cable-wrap limit (±270 degrees).
generated_requirements.rationale: Domain expertise identifies cable-wrap limit management as a mandatory scheduling constraint that must be enforced to prevent instrument damage.

generated_requirements.text: The telescope control software shall support non-sidereal tracking for solar system targets using ephemeris data in MPC two-line element or JPL Horizons differential-rate format; the maximum supported non-sidereal tracking rate shall be defined by the mount servo design; the autoguider shall compensate for guide star drift relative to the non-sidereal target.
generated_requirements.rationale: Domain expertise identifies non-sidereal tracking with autoguider compensation as a standard requirement for general-purpose observatory control systems.

generated_requirements.text: The dome azimuth, shutter state, and venting configuration shall be interlocked with telescope pointing limits and real-time wind speed thresholds; the software shall enforce, using a real-time geometric model of the dome-telescope relationship, that the telescope cannot be driven to any position where the optical beam is obstructed by the dome structure.
generated_requirements.rationale: Domain expertise identifies dome-telescope geometric interlock as a safety-critical constraint requiring a real-time model to prevent beam obstruction and equipment damage.

generated_requirements.text: Only one instrument shall have access to the telescope beam at any given time; inactive instruments shall be permitted to perform calibration exposures, flat-field exposures, and hot-standby preparation in parallel and independently of the active instrument and telescope operation.
generated_requirements.rationale: BR-IC-01 and BR-OM-07 define the single-active-instrument rule and the parallel operations permitted for inactive instruments.

generated_requirements.text: Instrument startup and shutdown shall be executable independently of the telescope and of other instruments, without affecting telescope operation or the operation of other mounted instruments.
generated_requirements.rationale: BR-IC-02 and OC-06 require instrument startup/shutdown independence as a fundamental architectural constraint.

generated_requirements.text: The visitor instrument interface shall provide a standardised subset protocol through which visitor instruments can: respond to status queries from upper system levels, accept preprogrammed observing sequences, and request telescope position offsets and focus adjustments; visitor instruments shall operate as servers responding to commands from the Gemini control hierarchy.
generated_requirements.rationale: BR-IC-06 defines the minimum required capabilities of the standardised visitor instrument interface.

generated_requirements.text: All astronomical data products shall conform to the current FITS standard (NASA/OSTA), including all mandatory and recommended keywords; World Coordinate System (WCS) headers shall be populated correctly for every detector readout, including distortion terms for mosaic focal planes.
generated_requirements.rationale: BR-DM-01 mandates FITS format; domain expertise identifies WCS header completeness including mosaic distortion terms as a non-negotiable archive and pipeline dependency.

generated_requirements.text: The UTC timestamp on each detector integration start and end shall be accurate to within 10 milliseconds of GPS-disciplined UTC, sourced from the site Local Time Bus; all system nodes shall maintain time synchronisation with a maximum inter-node skew of 10 milliseconds.
generated_requirements.rationale: Domain expertise identifies time-stamping accuracy and inter-node synchronisation as critical for data header integrity and astrometric reduction.

generated_requirements.text: All astronomical data shall be archived automatically during observing and maintenance operations; the system shall maintain at least two copies of all acquired data (one secured copy as acquired, one for on-line quality assessment); at least 7 days of data from the largest instrument shall be retained, with the most recent 3 days accessible interactively from hard disk.
generated_requirements.rationale: BR-DM-02, BR-DM-03, and BR-DM-04 define the dual-copy, automatic archiving, and retention requirements.

generated_requirements.text: For detector types requiring preprocessing (e.g. infrared array detectors), the system shall apply the required preprocessing pipeline and store only the preprocessed data product; raw intermediate data shall not be retained in the primary data store.
generated_requirements.rationale: BR-DM-06 specifies that only preprocessed data shall be stored for detectors requiring preprocessing, to manage storage capacity.

generated_requirements.text: Science data transmitted over LAN or WAN shall use only lossless compression; lossy compression (JPEG/MPEG) is permitted exclusively for rough video monitoring images transmitted at up to 0.5-second cadence; high-quality image transmission shall complete within 20 seconds.
generated_requirements.rationale: BR-DM-05 defines distinct compression policies for science data versus video monitoring to protect data integrity.

generated_requirements.text: The archive interface shall enforce time-based data access restrictions (proprietary periods) as defined by the Gemini data policy; the system shall not permit access to raw or reduced data by any authenticated user who does not hold programme ownership credentials, until the applicable proprietary period has expired.
generated_requirements.rationale: RC-02 and domain expertise on data embargo enforcement require the system to actively enforce proprietary period restrictions, not merely rely on archive-side controls.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall provide on-line quick-look data quality assessment tools, including image display, pixel statistics, and standard basic calibration reduction procedures, available during observations without interrupting the observing sequence.
generated_requirements.rationale: Objective 4 and Section 6.1 scope in on-line quick-look quality assessment as a required capability for real-time data evaluation.

generated_requirements.text: The user interface framework shall be homogeneous, network-transparent, and portable across all supported hardware platforms, presenting a consistent look and feel regardless of which subsystem is being operated or from which physical location the user is working.
generated_requirements.rationale: Section 4.2 and Section 6.1 require a unified, portable UI framework as a core architectural deliverable.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall display the current operational level (Observing, Maintenance, or Test) to all users at all times; incompatible mode combinations across subsystems shall be detected, flagged to operators, and prevented from executing.
generated_requirements.rationale: BR-OM-03 and BR-OM-04 require continuous operational level display and incompatible mode prevention.

generated_requirements.text: Every software module shall carry a version label retrievable at runtime via a standard control command; the system shall perform a boot-time consistency check verifying that all loaded module versions are mutually compatible according to the current release manifest, and shall refuse to enter observing mode if any inconsistency is detected.
generated_requirements.rationale: Objective 6 and Section 6.1 require runtime version labels and boot-time consistency checking to prevent operation with mismatched software versions.

generated_requirements.text: The on-line database shall be memory-resident and distributed; it shall provide access times in the range of 2–3 milliseconds per access for time-critical parameters; engineering data shall be loggable at up to 200 Hz for short-duration captures and at up to 1 Hz for long-term logging, stored in a common format (baselined as SYBASE).
generated_requirements.rationale: TC-06 and BR-MO-05 define the database access time and engineering data logging rate requirements.

generated_requirements.text: The upper-layer telescope control software shall be maintained as identical across both Gemini telescope sites (Hawaii and Chile); hardware differences between sites shall be accommodated exclusively at the IOC layer and below, with no impact on upper-layer software.
generated_requirements.rationale: BR-IC-07 and OC-05 mandate software identity across both sites to reduce maintenance burden and ensure operational consistency.

generated_requirements.text: All software shall be developed using the CVS version control system; on-line version control shall be available at all times during operations to recover or restore any previously released software version; a formal release system shall exist at package and module level, checkable on-line for consistency.
generated_requirements.rationale: TC-08, DC-01, and Objective 6 mandate CVS-based version control with on-line recovery capability and formal release management.

generated_requirements.text: Every software module shall have a corresponding test specification usable for both acceptance testing and on-line verification; regression test suites and self-test sequences shall be maintained for all packages.
generated_requirements.rationale: Objective 6 and DC-05 require per-module test specifications and regression suites to support ongoing verification.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall provide interface routines connecting the Gemini control system to external data reduction and analysis packages (IRAF, MIDAS, IDL, ADAM, Khoros, STARCAT, SYBASE/DBMS); these interface routines are part of the Gemini software system.
generated_requirements.rationale: Section 6.1 explicitly scopes in interface routines to external packages as part of the Gemini software system.

generated_requirements.text: Total system downtime attributable to software and control system failures shall not exceed 2% of available observing time (requirement), with a design goal of 1%; recovery from any single non-fatal subsystem failure shall return the telescope to an observing state within 5 minutes of onset of the error condition.
generated_requirements.rationale: Objective 2 and D-01 derive the 2%/1% downtime targets and 5-minute recovery time from the Gemini General System Requirements contractual obligations.

generated_requirements.text: Command handshaking between IOCs shall complete within 100–200 milliseconds; command acknowledgement timeouts shall be triggered at approximately 500 milliseconds; peak control information throughput shall support at least 100 transactions per second across the system.
generated_requirements.rationale: Objective 2 specifies these latency and throughput figures as measurable success criteria for real-time control performance.

generated_requirements.text: The system LAN shall support data transfer rates of 20–40 Mbits/second to accommodate concurrent multi-user data access; detector partial readout for focusing shall complete within approximately 0.1 seconds; full readout of large mosaicked optical detectors shall complete within 2–3 minutes.
generated_requirements.rationale: Objective 4 specifies LAN bandwidth and detector readout timing as measurable data throughput success criteria.

generated_requirements.text: Telescope cold-start (from powered-off state) shall complete within approximately 11 minutes (5 minutes software + 4 minutes telescope + 2 minutes instrument); warm restart excluding software download shall complete within approximately 1 minute.
generated_requirements.rationale: Objective 1 specifies startup timing as a measurable success criterion for minimising time-to-observing after interruptions.

generated_requirements.text: Monitoring activities shall not cause any measurable degradation in the performance of ongoing observations under any load condition, including peak ten-node operation.
generated_requirements.rationale: BR-MO-01 explicitly prohibits monitoring from affecting observation performance, which is a measurable performance constraint.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall support simultaneous operation of up to six active control nodes and two monitoring nodes without appreciable performance degradation; all operational capabilities shall remain available with up to ten active nodes under peak load.
generated_requirements.rationale: Objective 3 specifies the multi-node scalability requirement as a measurable success criterion for distributed operations.

generated_requirements.text: The user interface framework shall be portable across all supported hardware platforms and operating systems used at Gemini development and operational sites; no platform-specific code shall appear outside the IOC layer.
generated_requirements.rationale: TC-02 mandates machine-independent portable code for all software not directly controlling specific hardware.

generated_requirements.text: Addition of a new instrument to the system shall require no modification to existing operational software; all changes shall be confined to operational procedures, configuration files, and instrument-specific control modules.
generated_requirements.rationale: Objective 6 and OC-06 require that new instrument addition does not require modification of existing operational software.

generated_requirements.text: All equipment and software shall support a programmed adjustment and maintenance interval of 30 days or longer; the software shall not require any mandatory maintenance intervention more frequently than every 30 days.
generated_requirements.rationale: OC-01 and Objective 6 specify the 30-day maintenance interval as a measurable operational maintainability constraint.

generated_requirements.text: The number of distinct computer hardware platforms used across the system shall be kept to the minimum practical; all software not tied to specific hardware shall be written as portable, hardware-independent code to facilitate future platform migration.
generated_requirements.rationale: TC-10 and TC-02 mandate hardware platform minimisation and portability to reduce long-term maintenance burden.

generated_requirements.text: The access control system shall enforce authentication and authorisation for all user sessions; all access attempts, privilege escalations, and security-relevant events shall be logged with user identity, timestamp, and action taken.
generated_requirements.rationale: BR-AC-01 through BR-AC-09 collectively require a robust, auditable access control system; security logging is essential for an international multi-site facility.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall use commercial off-the-shelf packages, public domain software, and industry standards (including TCP/IP, NFS, RPC, FITS, EPICS) wherever feasible; proprietary, non-standard solutions shall not be used unless no standards-compliant alternative exists.
generated_requirements.rationale: TC-01 mandates standards compliance and avoidance of proprietary solutions to ensure long-term maintainability and interoperability.

generated_requirements.text: The user interface shall be designed to minimise operator fatigue and cognitive load during extended night-time observing sessions; interface elements shall provide unambiguous feedback on system state, and workflow design shall support team interaction between operators and observers.
generated_requirements.rationale: Section 6.1 and assumption A-11 scope in human engineering compliance covering fatigue, feedback, and team interaction as acceptance-testable usability requirements.

sme_subject: ground-based observatory operations engineer with experience in real-time telescope control systems and queue-based scheduling
sme_advisory: The Gemini control system sits at the intersection of hard real-time embedded control, distributed multi-site operations, and high-stakes scientific data integrity — a combination that demands requirements precision well beyond what a generalist systems engineer would naturally produce. The requirements engineer should pay particular attention to the boundary between the EPICS/VxWorks IOC layer and the upper-layer supervisory software, because timing guarantees, failure isolation, and simulation fidelity all behave very differently on either side of that boundary. Queue-based scheduling in a live observatory environment introduces subtle concurrency and preemption hazards that are not obvious from the BRD prose alone, and the safety architecture's layered interlock model must be traced all the way from hardware hard stops through to software watchdog heartbeat requirements to be complete and verifiable.

ground_truth_requirements.text: Definition Observing astronomers are the end users of the system. They range greatly in experience from occasional users of the system to very experienced users such as staff astronomers at the Gemini 8m Telescopes site and service observers. Purpose of work Whatever their level of experience, observing astronomers will wish to concentrate on the efficient acquisition of astronomical data and on-line assessment of data quality, rather than on the details of controlling the telescope and instruments. To allow observing astronomers to achieve this objective, the system must offer to a user an interface which, while fulfilling the various operational requirements in the different modes and offering status information both automatically and on request at any required level, is still simple to learn and secure in its use. Privileges Observing astronomers shall have no privileges as far as the direct control of the telescope is concerned. They shall not be able to send control commands directly but they must be able to enquire about the status of the telescope or any subsystem at any time. The intent is not to restrict the capabilities of the observing astronomer in any way but rather to establish a single point of control and responsibility. Programs, as opposed to observers, may have the capability of direct control of the telescope. This would allow the observer to create an observing program which requested a telescope control function but would not allow the observer to enter (for instance) a command to slew the mount. Astronomers are typically given control access to instruments, however. Operation mode Traditional interactive operation shall normally be replaced by operation via an automatic sequencer. This is essential to support operational requirements such as service observing and flexible scheduling (as defined later in Section 2.7 on page 2-12). A certain degree of interaction shall be provided, meaning in this case that the user will interact with the scheduler program, rather than with the control programs directly. Observing commands will normally be submitted via the User interface to a queue for later execution. It must also be possible to break and resequence this queue. e.g. as a result of the quality assessment of previous data. In particular, Operations staff will be able to enable direct interactive operation, but this shall not be considered as the normal operation mode for the reasons explained in Section 2.7 on page 2-12. It is evident that, for some functions (such as adjustment of spectrograph slit width for seeing conditions) it must be necessary to include interactive capability. However each instance of such a function should be examined as a candidate for automation - such as focussing. The existence of the scheduling queue shall be transparent to the on-site observer during the initial phases of telescope operation. Only after experience has been gained with the system will the existence of the queue become evident to the on site observer.
ground_truth_requirements.label: FR

ground_truth_requirements.text: Definition Currently, operations staff includes 'night assistants' and staff of the 'operations group'. Depending on the operations implemented on the Gemini 8m Telescopes, they may become telescope operators, operations supervisors of the Gemini 8m Telescopes telescope, and instrument operators and have to overview the Gemini 8m Telescopes operation. These users will be very experienced users of the system. They will be very familiar with the Gemini 8m Telescopes and/or its instruments. Purpose of work Operations staff will control the Gemini 8m Telescopes indirectly via a scheduler program or directly via commands. They will supervise telescope operation and will be able to advise observing astronomers on what they have to do to use telescope and instruments efficiently. They shall also monitor general performance and system safety. Privileges Operations staff shall have privileges to access all commands and maintenance procedures in case of problems. This includes direct control of physical units. However, they shall not have access to subsystems while these are in normal operation. If they need to access other parts of the system appropriate reconfiguration procedures have to be run. Operation mode Operations staff shall have access to operation tables in update mode, while observing astronomers will have access to them only in read mode (for example the list of filters mounted on an instrument). Again, the intent is not to restrict the capabilities of the observing astronomer in any way but rather to establish a single point of control and responsibility. At the present time it is not clear how to handle visitor instrument requirements in this area. They shall be able to change the operational status of units according to the results of tests performed on such units (e.g. to see if a faulty unit can be declared as operational again and redefined as part of the environment in use).
ground_truth_requirements.label: FR

ground_truth_requirements.text: Definition Software development and maintenance are staff: •based either at the Gemini 8m Telescopes site or base facility, or •based at the Gemini 8m Telescopes remote operations facility, or •non Gemini staff from a contract company or from an associated Institute. These users will have a deep understanding of the way the Gemini 8m Telescopes software works. Purpose of work Software development and maintenance staff intervene when there is a major problem to be solved or an upgrade to be installed. They also perform system generation and installation of new software packages or new releases, according to established test and validation procedures. Privileges Software development and maintenance staff need the highest privilege in order to be able to modify everything in the system. Nevertheless strict configuration control guidelines must be followed to prevent interference with ongoing normal operation. Operation mode Software development and maintenance staff usually work at the test level (see Section 3.2.1.3 on page 3-4) for the part of the software under test. Other parts of the Gemini 8m Telescopes may also need to run in test mode to support integration tests. This means that a number of commands are available which allow the hardware concerned to be tested (test commands) and are not accessible to observing astronomers.
ground_truth_requirements.label: FR

ground_truth_requirements.text: Normally three scientiﬁc instruments are mounted on the cassegrain focus of the telescope. The Gemini 8m Telescopes are thus characterized all the time as in a multi-instrument context. Parallel access to all the mounted instruments shall be provided, though only one instrument has access to the telescope beam (active instrument).Instruments which feed two detectors by using beam splitters or ﬁeld splitters is considered as a single active instrument. Feeding two separate instruments via beam splitters or ﬁeld splitters and providing separate control nodes is not a requirement. Various situations are possible for the other (inactive) instruments: Normally three scientiﬁc instruments are mounted on the cassegrain focus of the telescope. The Gemini 8m Telescopes are thus characterized all the time as in a multi-instrument context. Parallel access to all the mounted instruments shall be provided, though only one instrument has access to the telescope beam (active instrument).Instruments which feed two detectors by using beam splitters or ﬁeld splitters is considered as a single active instrument. Feeding two separate instruments via beam splitters or ﬁeld splitters and providing separate control nodes is not a requirement. Various situations are possible for the other (inactive) instruments: they shall be able to take calibration or ﬂat ﬁeld exposures in parallel they shall be able to prepare for an exposure to start as soon as the telescope beam is switched back to them (in this case, they are in a hot standby situation) they shall be able to work; at all foreseen operation levels (observing, maintenance, test) (see Section 3.2.1 on page 3-3).
ground_truth_requirements.label: FR

ground_truth_requirements.text: The requirement to provide for support, installation, and operation of outside instruments brought by the observer has several implications for the system. Due to the specialized nature of visitor instrumentation it is unlikely that complete integration into the Gemini environment is either feasible or warranted. In this case a subset of the available functionality must be made available through a standardized interface. The Gemini Telescopes view all instruments as operating as servers, responding to commands from the upper levels of the system. Visitor instruments must be capable of operating in this mode to be adequately supported. It should be the goal of this interface that it be a subset of the existing instrumentation interface (rather than a separate system). At a minimum this interface should support: The requirement to provide for support, installation, and operation of outside instruments brought by the observer has several implications for the system. Due to the specialized nature of visitor instrumentation it is unlikely that complete integration into the Gemini environment is either feasible or warranted. In this case a subset of the available functionality must be made available through a standardized interface. The Gemini Telescopes view all instruments as operating as servers, responding to commands from the upper levels of the system. Visitor instruments must be capable of operating in this mode to be adequately supported. It should be the goal of this interface that it be a subset of the existing instrumentation interface (rather than a separate system). At a minimum this interface should support: acquisition of status information of instrument by other systems capability to enter preprogrammed observing sequences capability to offset the telescope position and focus
ground_truth_requirements.label: FR

ground_truth_requirements.text: The requirements to have instruments operated as single units imply that several user stations will be active at the same time on the Gemini 8m Telescopes. On these user stations, different kinds of users may be working at the same time with the Gemini 8m Telescopes software. Independently of the location of users at the telescope site, they shall be able to access (according to their privileges) any part of the whole setup with a simple logon and conﬁguration operation. In other words. any subsection of the whole Gemini 8m Telescopes system should be accessible and controllable from any single point (but of course with protection ensuring security and safety). A particularly simple case of multi-use of the system is multi-point monitoring. By this it is meant that, while some (active) user is in control of the Gemini 8m Telescopes, someone else can follow what they are doing by monitoring all the relevant data from telescope and instruments. This will typically be needed by the operation supervisor. All other users wishing to monitor Gemini 8m Telescopes operations have to go through the procedures set up by Operations and get permission to do so. The multi-point monitoring mode might also be important when certain difﬁcult or rare problems occur, when expert advice is needed and can only be obtained from colleagues situated remotely. Multi-point monitoring allows them to follow directly the results of tests performed and investigate how the system is working (e.g. by selecting different display pages with the up-to-date status information on different parts of the system). Multi-point monitoring also allows a local observer to be monitored and advised by a remote supervisor. Monitoring shall exist both in the form of automatic displays of status information at different locations, and in the form of explicit access to the required status information from any point. Monitoring shall not affect the performance of ongoing observations. On these user stations, different kinds of users may be working at the same time with the Gemini 8m Telescopes software. Monitoring shall exist both in the form of automatic displays of status information at different locations, and in the form of explicit access to the required status information from any point. Monitoring shall not affect the performance of ongoing observations.
ground_truth_requirements.label: FR

ground_truth_requirements.text: Queue-based observing is the primary observation mode used with the Gemini telescopes, as it is the best means of providing sound science data while maximizing efﬁcient use of the telescope. While its requirements include all those given below for service observing, it has the following special requirements. Queue-based observing is the primary observation mode used with the Gemini telescopes, as it is the best means of providing sound science data while maximizing efﬁcient use of the telescope. While its requirements include all those given below for service observing, it has the following special requirements. The observing program must be fully automated, requiring very little human interaction during the observation. The means that the Gemini software must include a sufﬁciently rich programming environment to make this feasible. In addition, this should be a visually-oriented environment providing a simple, easy-to-use interface to the astronomer. There should be a full telescope simulator to enable the astronomer to test observing programs for completeness, errors, and functionality. This simulator should function within the virtual telescope environment of the Gemini system. All control software must provide support for simulated use within the virtual telescope There is a requirement for software to assist in object selection both within an observing program and across observing programs, in order to optimize observing efﬁciency. This software must consider target positions, weather conditions, and instrument conﬁgurations There must be software to support the ﬂexible scheduling, both manually and via a scheduler, allowing for the interleaving of observing programs in a manner that is transparent to the individual observing programs. This includes managing the collection of science, environmental, engineering, reference, and calibration data. To maximize the use of the available observing time it must be possible to queue all of the observing that is possible with the currently available instruments. These would be in the form of preprogrammed observing sequences. It should be possible to resort the queue so that the next observation to take place comes to the front of the queue. This sorting will be based on properties of the individual observing sequences, current site conditions, and other rules established by the observatory directorate.While these rules have yet to be worked out one could imagine rules such as “no more than 15% of observing will be preempted” or “a 10% overhead to the prime observing project is acceptable in order to pick up observations in similar parts of the sky with identical ﬁlters”.
ground_truth_requirements.label: FR

ground_truth_requirements.text: Remote observing means that users shall be able to observe from a remote site such as Cerro Tololo, Hilo or even home Institutes. This possibility has to be provided on the Gemini 8m Telescopes. The extent to which realistic observing conditions can be reproduced depends, of course, largely on the link bandwidth available. Experience with previous telescopes at other OBSERVING MODE REQUIREMENTS observatories shows, however, that, even with very limited bandwidths, remote observing can be implemented, provided the software is suitable for this. Remote observing, as meant in this context, does not mean remote control. Remote users shall normally interact with the system via Remote observing means that users shall be able to observe from a remote site such as Cerro Tololo, Hilo or even home Institutes. This possibility has to be provided on the Gemini 8m Telescopes. The extent to which realistic observing conditions can be reproduced depends, of course, largely on the link bandwidth available. Experience with previous telescopes at other OBSERVING MODE REQUIREMENTS observatories shows, however, that, even with very limited bandwidths, remote observing can be implemented, provided the software is suitable for this. Remote observing, as meant in this context, does not mean remote control. Remote users shall normally interact with the system via operators at the Gemini 8m Telescopes control facility operators at the Gemini 8m Telescopes site facility operators at the Gemini 8m Telescopes base facility operators at the Gemini 8m Telescopes remote operations facility via the scheduler program and shall not control any part of the Gemini 8m Telescopes directly.
ground_truth_requirements.label: FR

ground_truth_requirements.text: Quite apart from remote observing, remote access to the Gemini 8m Telescopes and its instruments is required for monitoring and diagnostic purposes. This might be necessary to back up local users expertise and to help in case of problems. Remote access, in this case, must be possible from the Gemini 8m Telescopes base facility. Distributed access to the Gemini 8m Telescopes software, once implemented. also allows, without extra requirements, local access (at the Gemini 8m Telescopes site) or remote access (Gemini 8m Telescopes support, base, and remote operations facilities). Quite apart from remote observing, remote access to the Gemini 8m Telescopes and its instruments is required for monitoring and diagnostic purposes. This might be necessary to back up local users expertise and to help in case of problems. Remote access, in this case, must be possible from the Gemini 8m Telescopes base facility. Distributed access to the Gemini 8m Telescopes software, once implemented. also allows, without extra requirements, local access (at the Gemini 8m Telescopes site) or remote access (Gemini 8m Telescopes support, base, and remote operations facilities).
ground_truth_requirements.label: FR

ground_truth_requirements.text: Service observing implies that the Gemini staff are responsible for performing the actual data collection for the astronomer. A special case of service observing, queue-based, is given earlier, and assumes that the observing program can be executed with minimal interaction with the client astronomer. The more general case, where a science observer is present on site and the client astronomer is remotely connected, is considered here. The requirements speciﬁc to service observing are: Service observing implies that the Gemini staff are responsible for performing the actual data collection for the astronomer. A special case of service observing, queue-based, is given earlier, and assumes that the observing program can be executed with minimal interaction with the client astronomer. The more general case, where a science observer is present on site and the client astronomer is remotely connected, is considered here. The requirements speciﬁc to service observing are: The observing program must be automated, requiring little human interaction during the observation. The means that the Gemini software must include a sufﬁciently rich programming environment to make this feasible. In addition, this should be a visually-oriented environment providing a simple, easy-to-use interface to the astronomer. This programming environment should be available both to the astronomer, for developing the program, and to the observer, for review and adjustment of the program. This access may or may not be done concurrently on a shared environment. The programming environment should allow for the communication of special notes, instructions, and comments from the astronomer to the observer, possibly involving multimedia techniques. The use of this feature would, however, be discouraged for queue-based observing.
ground_truth_requirements.label: FR

ground_truth_requirements.text: To achieve efﬁcient service observing, queue observing, and ﬂexible scheduling, it should be possible to carry out observations automatically, in accordance with predeﬁned sequences of exposures, as is commonly done in space observatories. This corresponds to what is meant by planned observing, which still requires competent monitoring at the Gemini 8m Telescopes site or remotely. At the same time, one does not want to lose the advantages and the extra ﬂexibility of ground-based astronomy. So whichever scheme is adopted to perform automatic sequences, interaction shall be allowed at the desired level (for example, only on error conditions, any time the user is allowed to break a sequence, etc.). To achieve efﬁcient service observing, queue observing, and ﬂexible scheduling, it should be possible to carry out observations automatically, in accordance with predeﬁned sequences of exposures, as is commonly done in space observatories. This corresponds to what is meant by planned observing, which still requires competent monitoring at the Gemini 8m Telescopes site or remotely. At the same time, one does not want to lose the advantages and the extra ﬂexibility of ground-based astronomy. So whichever scheme is adopted to perform automatic sequences, interaction shall be allowed at the desired level (for example, only on error conditions, any time the user is allowed to break a sequence, etc.).
ground_truth_requirements.label: FR

ground_truth_requirements.text: Flexible scheduling means the possibility of reacting to changes in weather and other conditions by allocating the current use of the telescope to the optimal observing program for those conditions. It will require the use of service and/or remote observing, as users cannot stay around indeﬁnitely waiting for the conditions required by their program. This can be complemented by remote monitoring, if users can be on standby at a suitably equipped remote site. Flexible scheduling requires the possibility to change telescope scheduling quickly, exchanging observing programs. This can be achieved only with the help of appropriate scheduling software, which must advise the operations team on the best choice to make which is compatible with the relative priorities of the various observing programs. It should be recognized that such ‘expert scheduling’ software does not currently exist - it should be a requirement of such software that it have an operational mode which merely supports the decision making process by presenting appropriate information. Flexible scheduling requires switching during the night among telescope modes and instruments (typically 2 times per night). Thus ﬂexible scheduling is a superset of queue scheduling, with similar requirements. Flexible scheduling means the possibility of reacting to changes in weather and other conditions by allocating the current use of the telescope to the optimal observing program for those conditions. It will require the use of service and/or remote observing, as users cannot stay around indeﬁnitely waiting for the conditions required by their program. This can be complemented by remote monitoring, if users can be on standby at a suitably equipped remote site. Flexible scheduling requires the possibility to change telescope scheduling quickly, exchanging observing programs. This can be achieved only with the help of appropriate scheduling software, which must advise the operations team on the best choice to make which is compatible with the relative priorities of the various observing programs. It should be recognized that such ‘expert scheduling’ software does not currently exist - it should be a requirement of such software that it have an operational mode which merely supports the decision making process by presenting appropriate information. Flexible scheduling requires switching during the night among telescope modes and instruments (typically 2 times per night). Thus ﬂexible scheduling is a superset of queue scheduling, with similar requirements.
ground_truth_requirements.label: FR

ground_truth_requirements.text: The syntax of control ﬂow commands is to be consistent across the system, whether accessing workstation software or IOC software. See Section 3.2.5 on page 3-6. All subsystems must respond to a common set of commands to test operational status, inquiries as to version, perform self-tests, etc. These common commands are described in Section 5.3 on page 5-2. All IOC subsystems must respond to additional common commands for such activities as start, stop, initialize, reset parameters, etc. These IOC common commands are described in Section 5.3 on page 5-2. The support structure for communicating commands must be reliable, with a uniform ACK/NAK protocol adopted across all systems. Timeouts must be supported at approximately 500 msec. Handshaking of commands between IOCs must occur within 100-200 msec, signaling acceptance of each command. For commands allowing delayed replies, timeouts for that reply must also be supported. Peak control information within the system is expected to be 100 TPS. This assumes bridging between communication sections, to isolate trafﬁc in relevant sections only.
ground_truth_requirements.label: FR

ground_truth_requirements.text: Data ﬂow. Data from detectors must be stored in the most effective method permitted by available technology. Astronomical data is often detector readout limited so that disk access and data transfer times are not signiﬁcant. Maximum acceptable readout time for detector data is very dependent on detector type and size as well as the intended application: For focusing and related activities, maximum acceptable detector readout time is about 0.1 sec, though only a portion of the detector may be read during that time. For mosaicked, large optical detectors, a full readout of the detector must be done in about 2 or 3 minutes. Concurrent data access and display. Since the Gemini system supports monitoring of operation, there must be the capability of providing multiple, simultaneous access to data. Data transfer between the virtual telescope system and attached workstations therefore imposes signiﬁcant transfer requirements on the LAN. The LAN must support a transfer rate of 20-40 Mbits/second. Data acquisition format. Data is normally acquired as uncompressed data, but may be compressed using a loss-less compression technique for transmission from the Gemini system or across the system LAN. The goal of compression is to minimize bandwidth impact on the LAN and WAN and to save space on removable media. For data that requires preprocessing, such as infrared detector data, only the preprocessed data is stored. Storage of data. Data from all instruments and detectors is stored as compressed data, using a standard format. There is a ﬁrst level of storage within IOCs, to secure data in the event of link failures.A second level of storage is on the Gemini system data disk(s), possibly also on removable media. Quick-look data quality assessment is done using this level. Archiving of data is automatically done while in observing and maintenance level operation to the Gemini Archive subsystem. Shipping of data to a central archive follows later. Data transmission format. Data is transmitted between Gemini and home Institutes using a FITS format and contains all header information provided with the data. System-wide data capacity. The data capacity of the system is limited by transfer methods and technology, as well as archiving capacity on site. The system data capacity is capable of retaining 7 days of data produced by the largest instrument, the last 3 days of which must be available interactively from hard disk or similar medium.
ground_truth_requirements.label: FR

ground_truth_requirements.text: Video information originates from target acquisition, guiding, and site monitoring cameras. The requirements for transferring video data are: Video information originates from target acquisition, guiding, and site monitoring cameras. The requirements for transferring video data are: The system must allow for fast transmission of rough images every 0.5 sec. This may be assisted through the use of data-loss compression techniques (e.g. JPEG, MPEG, etc). In addition, there is the need for transmission of images matching the original resolution. This high-quality transmission must require less than 20 sec, and can only be assisted with loss-less compression.
ground_truth_requirements.label: FR

ground_truth_requirements.text: To preserve the integrity of the system, there must be a system of privileges established at each operating level of the system. These privileges should be determined in a simple manner during logging into the system. Protection against accidental interference is to be implemented using an Access Mode Allocation system that dynamically identiﬁes and assigns resources as needed. Critical resources (those that can support only a restricted number of simultaneous uses) are assigned solely through this allocation system. The allocation system must ensure that the system cannot remain deadlocked with respect to this resource allocation. Finally, procedures must be implemented for convenience and system integrity, to simplify and codify common tasks. The tasks that require such procedures include: To preserve the integrity of the system, there must be a system of privileges established at each operating level of the system. These privileges should be determined in a simple manner during logging into the system. Protection against accidental interference is to be implemented using an Access Mode Allocation system that dynamically identiﬁes and assigns resources as needed. Critical resources (those that can support only a restricted number of simultaneous uses) are assigned solely through this allocation system. The allocation system must ensure that the system cannot remain deadlocked with respect to this resource allocation. Finally, procedures must be implemented for convenience and system integrity, to simplify and codify common tasks. The tasks that require such procedures include: Telescope start-up and shutdown Telescope system self-testing. Instrument start-up and shut-down. This is not permitted to interfere with telescope operation. Instrument self-testing and self-diagnosis This is not permitted to interfere with telescope operation Conﬁguration and reconﬁguration Dynamic reconﬁguration of observing conﬁguration (beam switching without restarting instruments and telescope). The control software should know what subsystems are installed and their status at all times.
ground_truth_requirements.label: FR

ground_truth_requirements.text: The telescope and instrument software shall contain built-in test (BIT) facilities to verify Gemini 8m Telescopes system and Gemini 8m Telescopes software performances. Every Gemini 8m Telescopes software module shall have corresponding test speciﬁcations to check normal operation of releases, to be used both for acceptance tests and as an on-line test procedure. The Gemini 8m Telescopes control software shall also provide for execution of self-test sequences of the Gemini 8m Telescopes system and subsystems. These shall automatically exercise all subsystems present in a given operational conﬁguration. Regression tests should be a part of every Gemini 8m Telescopes software package The telescope and instrument software shall contain built-in test (BIT) facilities to verify Gemini 8m Telescopes system and Gemini 8m Telescopes software performances. Every Gemini 8m Telescopes software module shall have corresponding test speciﬁcations to check normal operation of releases, to be used both for acceptance tests and as an on-line test procedure. The Gemini 8m Telescopes control software shall also provide for execution of self-test sequences of the Gemini 8m Telescopes system and subsystems. These shall automatically exercise all subsystems present in a given operational conﬁguration. Regression tests should be a part of every Gemini 8m Telescopes software package
ground_truth_requirements.label: FR

ground_truth_requirements.text: Subsystems must notify the user when faults occur. This notiﬁcation must be speciﬁc as to origin and problem. The notiﬁcation must also be capable of being electronically logged. It may also prove useful to have multiple levels of fault notiﬁcation such as detailed, verbose, short, etc. to aid in tracking down problems. Subsystems must notify the user when faults occur. This notiﬁcation must be speciﬁc as to origin and problem. The notiﬁcation must also be capable of being electronically logged. It may also prove useful to have multiple levels of fault notiﬁcation such as detailed, verbose, short, etc. to aid in tracking down problems.
ground_truth_requirements.label: FR

ground_truth_requirements.text: Should a subsystem fail (e.g. one detector, one instrument) predeﬁned procedures must exist to redeﬁne the environment in such a way that operation can restart with the remaining equipment. In case of computer hardware failure concerning the user station equipment, it shall be possible to transfer control from one user station to another via a simple software reconﬁguration procedure. In the case of IOC failure, no transfer of control to another IOC will be possible, due to the local connections and interfaces to the control electronics. In this case there shall be a procedure to replace faulty cards and/or assemblies. If it is possible to observe with that particular IOC in a failed state (in general, this is limited to IOCs that are associated with individual scientiﬁc instruments) then it must be possible to reconﬁgure the system to do so. Should a subsystem fail (e.g. one detector, one instrument) predeﬁned procedures must exist to redeﬁne the environment in such a way that operation can restart with the remaining equipment. In case of computer hardware failure concerning the user station equipment, it shall be possible to transfer control from one user station to another via a simple software reconﬁguration procedure. In the case of IOC failure, no transfer of control to another IOC will be possible, due to the local connections and interfaces to the control electronics. In this case there shall be a procedure to replace faulty cards and/or assemblies. If it is possible to observe with that particular IOC in a failed state (in general, this is limited to IOCs that are associated with individual scientiﬁc instruments) then it must be possible to reconﬁgure the system to do so.
ground_truth_requirements.label: FR

ground_truth_requirements.text: Full redundancy is not a requirement of the Gemini 8m Telescopes and it will be acceptable to have to replace units in case of failure. There are subsystems which are relatively inexpensive to support as redundant systems, such as telescope control computers. For each area where redundancy is decided to be cost effective, procedures for switching to the backup system will be established. There is no requirement for automatic switching to the backup system. All communication shall be based on the use of standard communication protocols, where retry procedures are applied (a form of software redundancy) as part of the protocol. Certain network concepts may be preferable as they offer intrinsic redundancy (e.g. double loops) and re-routing possibilities in case of node failures (single point failure protection). Full redundancy is not a requirement of the Gemini 8m Telescopes and it will be acceptable to have to replace units in case of failure. There are subsystems which are relatively inexpensive to support as redundant systems, such as telescope control computers. For each area where redundancy is decided to be cost effective, procedures for switching to the backup system will be established. There is no requirement for automatic switching to the backup system. All communication shall be based on the use of standard communication protocols, where retry procedures are applied (a form of software redundancy) as part of the protocol. Certain network concepts may be preferable as they offer intrinsic redundancy (e.g. double loops) and re-routing possibilities in case of node failures (single point failure protection).
ground_truth_requirements.label: FR

ground_truth_requirements.text: There are a number of general constraints placed on the Gemini Software. These are: There are a number of general constraints placed on the Gemini Software. These are: Commercial packages, off-the-shelf public domain software, and standards are to be used whenever feasible. Existing external software will be integrated with the Gemini software. The interfaces involved in this integration are considered part of the Gemini software system. All Gemini software is to be developed using standard methodologies and development environments. One of the goals of Gemini software is that all components be easily (preferably automatically) combined into an integrated system. Gemini software developers should maintain accurate change logs showing software modiﬁcations as they are applied to the system software. Gemini software developers should adhere to a standard method for the reporting and recording of errors from both internal and external sources. Gemini software should be developed in evolutionary fashion, using the CVS version control system All Gemini subsystem packages should include as part of the software both a simulator module for inclusion in the virtual telescope (see Design constraints, below), and user interface modules for the user interface environments that the subsystem will be operating in. The interfaces required of these user interface modules are described in Section 3.3 on page 3-6. All Gemini software is to be fully documented, internally with appropriate comments, and external documentation. External documentation must include Unix-style man pages. All Gemini subsystem packages must provide modules for the testing and diagnosis of the subsystem. All instrumentation control software must provide full access to all instrument functionality. It is likely that different user interface modules (see above) would present different portions of this functionality to the user. The information required of each interface module is found in the Functional Requirements speciﬁcations for each instrument. All Gemini software must be version labeled, both in source and binary form. The version information is to be retrievable from executing software via control commands.
ground_truth_requirements.label: FR

ground_truth_requirements.text: .There are different requirements for software running on different layers. For example, strict real-time control is restricted to the IOC layer. Also, the use of a virtual-telescope model in the Gemini system means that the integrated system can be tested and developed independently of the target hardware. This is useful not only in the use of the telescope simulator during science planning, but in maintenance and testing as well. Therefore all hardware subsystems must provide a software simulation module (as stated earlier) that responds in reasonable fashion to commands directed at that hardware. This simulation may require a standard environment, such as VxWorks, EPICS, and VME crate/cpu, but it cannot require any hardware speciﬁc to the application. .Gemini subsystem should be as self-contained and autonomous as possible, thereby decreasing the functional width of the interface to the rest of the Gemini system. No subsystem package should make any assumptions about the surrounding environment beyond that provided in the interface speciﬁcations.
ground_truth_requirements.label: FR

ground_truth_requirements.text: Control information must be transferred, typically in the form of commands and replies from users, to telescope and instruments. Replies might contain status information and, in general, data concerning instruments and telescopes, to be stored together with the astronomical data. Control information on all controlled variables must be provided by all subsystems on request. No request for information shall produce a delay of control activities or locking, even if the corresponding equipment is not available or faulty. Delay times for the exchange of control information must stay within precise time limits to be deﬁned in “General Description” in Chapter 2. One can afford to retransmit commands in case of transmission error or collision, but the protocol has to be predictable in that commands cannot get lost and replies have to come back reliably. In a number of cases, synchronization with the Time Reference System at the Gemini 8m Telescopes site is also necessary. Access to control parameters, telescope and instrument information for monitoring or other use makes a signiﬁcant contribution to the control ﬂow, and may be logged at quite high rates for short periods (i.e. up to 200 Hz for some information). It is explicitly required that all such information is available to the Gemini 8m Telescopes software and is capable of being available to all users of the Gemini 8m Telescopes, subject only to restrictions with respect to updating. It must also be possible to restrict user access to such information. In particular also, the meteorological information coming from a weather station should be available centrally. ground_truth_requirements.label: FR

ground_truth_requirements.text: Detector data must be acquired and stored in the most effective way technology will allow; effectiveness should be evaluated in terms of cost, space requirements, longevity, and speed. This shall lead to the deﬁnition of a Gemini 8m Telescopes standard, used on all instruments. In general, operational overheads must be kept as low as possible, to maximize actual observing times. Intermediate storage of raw data in memory on different nodes and in different formats should be kept to a minimum. However, there must be at least two copies - one to secure data as acquired and one to do assessment of data quality on-line (this last copy preferably on removable media). The link chosen to transfer data should represent as small a bottleneck as possible for data acquisition. The link chosen to transfer data should represent as small a bottleneck as possible for data acquisition.

ground_truth_requirements.text: TV data concerning site monitoring and voice need to be capable of being available at all operations facilities. It will be a question of interfacing and bandwidth costs whether such information is actually available at a speciﬁc location. It is not a requirement that point to point video be available between Gemini 8m Telescopes operations facilities. It is a requirement that voice connectivity, perhaps point to point, be available on a permanent connection. Other astronomical information such as that coming from sky ﬁeld monitors, autoguider cameras and sky monitoring devices such as cloud and seeing monitors shall also be capable of being available. ground_truth_requirements.text: This allows the installation and testing of new packages or new releases. Any low level test can be performed in this mode. It shall be possible to update all non-protected parameter values, i.e. those not used by operations at observing level. Access to this level will be extremely limited. ground_truth_requirements.text: Protections must also be enforced (see also Security in Section 3.5.6 on page 3- 20) among users and the operational software should indicate clearly to users the current operation level and check the compatibility between subsystems in different modes. ground_truth_requirements.text: The capacity of the system can be expressed in terms of nodes, which is deﬁned as the number of workstations, or in terms of users, which is deﬁned as the sum total of users at all the nodes. The capacity requirements will be expressed in terms of nodes. Each node will have the capability to run at all operation levels. When the Gemini 8m Telescopes telescope is used in its normal observing mode, there will be a single operator node for the telescope and two data acquisition and instrument control nodes. OPERATION Some tests might be run in parallel on instruments that do not have the light beam at that moment, so in principle additional nodes might be working at the same time. The system will provide for one auxiliary data acquisition and instrument control nodes. In addition, the system must support off-site observing modes. The system will provide for a single off-site data acquisition and instrument control node - to be located at either the Gemini 8m Telescopes Site Support or Base Facility. One supervisor will monitor the system, and other users might need to monitor the running of observing programs, locally or remotely. The system will provide for a single local monitoring node and a single remote monitoring node. As a conclusion, the Gemini 8m Telescopes control software shall allow simultaneous operation of up to six active control nodes and up to two more monitoring nodes (one local and one remote) without appreciable degradation of performance. In practice the operation and facilities foreseen so far for the Gemini 8m Telescopes will limit this number to a maximum in the order of three active nodes, but the Gemini 8m Telescopes computers and software shall be capable of coping with the load of 10 active nodes, should the case arise The capacity of the system can be expressed in terms of nodes, which is deﬁned as the number of workstations, or in terms of users, which is deﬁned as the sum total of users at all the nodes.
