generated_requirements.text: The system shall define and enforce a standard message format for service information descriptions exchanged between CDN components; any message that does not conform to this format must be rejected and the rejection event logged with timestamp, source identifier, and reason code.
generated_requirements.rationale: BR-1 and BO-7 mandate zero interoperability failures attributable to format inconsistencies, requiring strict format validation at every interface boundary.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall define and enforce a standard message format for service requirement messages exchanged between CDN components; any non-conforming service requirement message must be rejected and logged before any processing occurs.
generated_requirements.rationale: BR-1 requires all service requirement messages to conform to defined standard formats, with non-conforming messages rejected and logged.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall implement a defined interaction protocol for the Web Server to Service Router (SR) interface, specifying message types, sequencing, error codes, and timeout behavior for all exchanges at that boundary.
generated_requirements.rationale: BR-2 and the future-state architecture require fully defined protocols at every inter-component boundary, including Web Server↔SR.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall implement a defined interaction protocol for the Service Router (SR) to Mediator interface, specifying message types, sequencing, error codes, and timeout behavior for all exchanges at that boundary.
generated_requirements.rationale: BR-2 mandates protocol adherence at the SR↔Mediator boundary, which is the primary path for escalating capacity requests to the negotiation layer.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall implement a defined interaction protocol for the Mediator to Peer Router (PR) interface, specifying message types, sequencing, error codes, and timeout behavior for all exchanges at that boundary.
generated_requirements.rationale: BR-2 requires defined protocols at the Mediator↔PR boundary to enable automated peer CDN resource acquisition.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall implement a defined interaction protocol for the Mediator to Policy Agent (PA) interface, specifying message types, sequencing, error codes, and timeout behavior for all exchanges at that boundary.
generated_requirements.rationale: BR-8 requires the Mediator to work in conjunction with the PA for all negotiation commitments, necessitating a well-defined Mediator↔PA protocol.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall implement a defined interaction protocol for the Policy Agent to Policy Agent (PA↔PA) interface across CDN domain boundaries, specifying message types, sequencing, trust validation steps, error codes, and timeout behavior.
generated_requirements.rationale: The PA↔PA interface is the inter-domain trust boundary and must have a fully specified protocol to enable automated long-term policy exchange and validation.

generated_requirements.text: At the establishment of every inter-component session, each peer component must advertise its supported protocol version(s); the system must select the highest mutually supported version and must reject the session with a descriptive error if no common version exists, without silently falling back to an undefined behavior.
generated_requirements.rationale: Heterogeneous CDN environments will run different software generations simultaneously; version negotiation prevents silent protocol mismatches that could block entire peering workflows.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall support a short-term peering negotiation workflow that completes — from request initiation through resource activation confirmation — within 60 seconds under normal operating conditions.
generated_requirements.rationale: BO-2 and TC-3 set a hard target of sub-60-second short-term negotiation to enable real-time demand response during traffic surges.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall implement a short-term peering negotiation workflow that includes the following ordered steps: (1) capacity shortfall detection by the SR, (2) negotiation request submission to the Mediator, (3) PA policy authorization, (4) peer CDN selection and negotiation execution, (5) resource activation, and (6) confirmation returned to the SR.
generated_requirements.rationale: BO-2 and BR-8 require a fully automated, end-to-end short-term negotiation workflow with PA involvement at the authorization step.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall implement a long-term peering arrangement workflow that stores, versions, retrieves, and automatically applies machine-readable peering policies without requiring human intervention for each policy application event.
generated_requirements.rationale: BO-3 requires long-term peering policies to be machine-readable, versioned, and applied automatically.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall classify every peering arrangement as either short-term or long-term at the time of initiation and shall apply the distinct negotiation procedures, policy sets, and timing constraints defined for that classification throughout the arrangement's lifecycle.
generated_requirements.rationale: BR-9 mandates that each peering arrangement classification has distinct procedures that must be applied accordingly.

generated_requirements.text: The Mediator component must not commit to any peering arrangement — short-term or long-term — without receiving explicit authorization from the Policy Agent; any negotiation message that would constitute a commitment must be held pending PA authorization and must be cancelled if authorization is not received within a configurable timeout.
generated_requirements.rationale: BR-8 prohibits the Mediator from unilaterally committing to a peering arrangement without PA involvement.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall implement a policy engine that evaluates resource provisioning, delegation, and reservation requests against applicable policies before any external resource is allocated; every allocation decision must be recorded with a reference to the specific policy that authorized it.
generated_requirements.rationale: BR-3 requires that no external resource be provisioned without an authorizing policy and that all allocation decisions be traceable to a specific policy.

generated_requirements.text: The policy engine shall support the definition, storage, retrieval, update, and revocation of at least four policy types: functional policies, peering policies, SLA policies, and renegotiation policies; each policy must carry a unique identifier, version number, effective date, and expiry date.
generated_requirements.rationale: BR-13 and the scope require a comprehensive policy framework covering all governance dimensions of peering arrangements.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall allow policy changes — including new policy deployment, policy updates, and policy revocations — to take effect without requiring system downtime or interruption of active peering arrangements.
generated_requirements.rationale: OC-3 explicitly requires that policy changes be deployable without system downtime.

generated_requirements.text: When multiple applicable policies (functional, SLA, security) produce conflicting directives for the same resource or negotiation event, the system must resolve the conflict using a deterministic, pre-defined precedence hierarchy and must log the conflict, the policies involved, and the resolution outcome.
generated_requirements.rationale: Overlapping policy scopes are inevitable in a multi-policy engine; a deterministic precedence hierarchy prevents unpredictable system behavior.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall implement automated SLA monitoring that continuously measures the following metrics for each active peering arrangement: Time to First Byte (TTFB), throughput, error rate, and cache-hit ratio; measurements must be taken at defined sampling intervals not exceeding 30 seconds and aggregated over defined windows aligned with SLA reporting periods.
generated_requirements.rationale: BO-4 and D-6 require real-time SLA monitoring with formally defined metrics, measurement points, and aggregation windows to constitute SLA compliance evidence.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall automatically detect SLA violations by comparing measured performance metrics against the SLA thresholds defined in the applicable SLA policy; a violation must be flagged within one SLA measurement aggregation window of the breach occurring.
generated_requirements.rationale: BO-4 requires automated SLA violation detection to trigger consequence and renegotiation workflows within defined timeframes.

generated_requirements.text: Upon detection of an SLA violation, the system shall automatically apply the consequences defined in the applicable SLA violation policy and initiate a renegotiation procedure as specified by the applicable renegotiation policy, without requiring manual escalation.
generated_requirements.rationale: BR-11 and BR-12 require that SLA violation consequences be pre-defined and automatically applied, and that renegotiation be triggered automatically.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall escalate to manual intervention only if automated renegotiation fails after a configurable maximum number of attempts; the escalation event must be logged and a notification sent to the designated operations contact.
generated_requirements.rationale: BR-12 specifies that manual escalation is only required if automated renegotiation fails after a defined number of attempts.

generated_requirements.text: Automated renegotiation workflows must not modify financial exposure — such as committed data rates or SLA penalty thresholds — beyond pre-authorized policy limits; any renegotiation action that would exceed those limits must be blocked and escalated for human authorization.
generated_requirements.rationale: Fully autonomous financial commitment beyond defined thresholds requires contractual guardrails to prevent unauthorized financial liability.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall verify that all required content has been successfully replicated to the designated Web servers before activating any peering arrangement for content delivery; activation must be blocked and an error returned if replication is not confirmed.
generated_requirements.rationale: BR-6 prohibits peering activation without confirmed content replication to prevent serving stale or missing content through peer CDN nodes.

generated_requirements.text: Content replication to peer CDN nodes must be blocked for any jurisdiction where the content provider's SLA does not grant distribution rights; the system must check geo-restriction and content licensing constraints embedded in the content provider's SLA before initiating or confirming replication.
generated_requirements.rationale: Replicating content to jurisdictions where distribution rights are not held exposes the operator to copyright and licensing liability.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall implement a malicious request detection mechanism at all peering interfaces that evaluates incoming requests against defined threat intelligence rules or signatures before any negotiation processing occurs; requests identified as malicious must be rejected immediately without initiating any negotiation step or system state change.
generated_requirements.rationale: BR-5 and BO-5 require that malicious requests be rejected before they can affect resource allocation or negotiation state.

generated_requirements.text: The malicious request detection mechanism must achieve a false-negative rate below 1% as measured against a defined test corpus of known malicious request patterns; this rate must be validated at initial deployment and re-validated whenever threat intelligence rules are updated.
generated_requirements.rationale: BO-5 sets a hard KPI of fewer than 1% false negatives for malicious request detection to protect CDN infrastructure from abuse.

generated_requirements.text: The malicious request detection mechanism must complete its evaluation and either pass or reject a request within a latency budget that does not cause the total short-term negotiation workflow to exceed the 60-second SLA window; the maximum detection latency must be documented as part of the system's latency budget decomposition.
generated_requirements.rationale: TC-4 requires that security mechanisms operate with sufficiently low latency to not introduce unacceptable delay into the negotiation workflow.

generated_requirements.text: Every rejected malicious request must be logged with the following attributes: timestamp (from a trusted time source), source IP address, request content summary, matched threat rule identifier, and the identity of the rejecting component; these logs must be retained for a minimum of 3 years or the period required by applicable cybersecurity regulations, whichever is longer.
generated_requirements.rationale: BR-5 and RC-3 require audit logging of all rejected malicious requests with retention sufficient to meet regulatory requirements such as the NIS2 Directive.

generated_requirements.text: All inter-CDN communication at peering interfaces must be authenticated using mutual TLS (mTLS); the system must validate the peer's certificate against a trusted certificate authority before processing any message, and must reject connections from peers presenting expired, revoked, or untrusted certificates.
generated_requirements.rationale: Mutual authentication at inter-domain boundaries is essential to prevent spoofed resource requests and unauthorized peering session establishment.

generated_requirements.text: The system must implement certificate lifecycle management for mTLS peering connections, including: automated certificate rotation before expiry, real-time revocation checking via OCSP or CRL, and a defined procedure for handling certificate revocation events during active peering sessions without dropping in-flight content delivery.
generated_requirements.rationale: Certificate expiry or revocation during an active peering session could silently degrade or terminate content delivery if not explicitly handled.

generated_requirements.text: All SLA audit records must be timestamped using a trusted, externally verifiable time source (e.g., NTP synchronized to a stratum-1 source or a qualified trust service provider); records must be stored in a tamper-evident format and retained for a minimum of 7 years or the applicable statute of limitations under the governing law of the peering agreement, whichever is longer.
generated_requirements.rationale: RC-2 requires SLA audit records to satisfy evidentiary standards for contractual dispute resolution, necessitating tamper-evidence, trusted timestamps, and jurisdiction-appropriate retention.

generated_requirements.text: The system must detect and report any resource failure within an active peering arrangement to all affected parties within 30 seconds of failure detection; batch or delayed failure reporting is not permitted for active peering arrangements.
generated_requirements.rationale: BR-4 and OC-2 require near-real-time resource failure reporting; silent degradation of active peering arrangements is explicitly prohibited.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall maintain content availability at or above 99.9% uptime during active peering engagements, measured as the percentage of time during which content is successfully deliverable through the peering arrangement.
generated_requirements.rationale: BO-6 sets a hard KPI of ≥ 99.9% content availability during peering engagements.

generated_requirements.text: The Mediator component must be capable of managing concurrent peering negotiations with multiple peer CDNs simultaneously; the system must not serialize concurrent negotiation requests, and must define the maximum supported concurrency level as a configurable parameter.
generated_requirements.rationale: TC-3 explicitly prohibits serialization bottlenecks in the Mediator when handling concurrent negotiations with multiple peer CDNs.

generated_requirements.text: All negotiation message handlers must be idempotent: duplicate or replayed negotiation messages — such as those arising from retransmission over lossy links — must not result in double-allocation of resources, duplicate peering sessions, or any unintended state change; the system must detect and discard duplicate messages using a message identifier and sequence number scheme.
generated_requirements.rationale: Retransmission over internet infrastructure can produce duplicate messages; idempotent handling prevents resource over-commitment and duplicate session creation.

generated_requirements.text: The system must define and implement partial-failure and rollback semantics for the short-term negotiation workflow: if any step in the negotiation sequence fails after resources have been partially allocated, the system must automatically release all partially allocated resources and return all affected components to their pre-negotiation state.
generated_requirements.rationale: Stateful negotiation sessions spanning multiple components require explicit rollback semantics to prevent resource leaks and inconsistent state on partial failure.

generated_requirements.text: The system must implement a graceful peering teardown workflow for both short-term and long-term arrangements that includes: (1) notification to all participating components, (2) DNS steering reversion, (3) content cache invalidation at peer CDN nodes, (4) resource quota release, and (5) confirmation of teardown completion logged with timestamp.
generated_requirements.rationale: Orderly termination of peering arrangements is required to reclaim resources, prevent stale DNS entries, and avoid serving cached content after arrangement expiry.

generated_requirements.text: The system must specify and implement the traffic steering mechanism used to redirect user requests to peer CDN resources upon peering activation, including at minimum: DNS TTL manipulation, Anycast prefix advertisement, or HTTP redirect chains; the maximum propagation latency for each supported mechanism must be documented and must fit within the overall 60-second short-term negotiation budget.
generated_requirements.rationale: Peering activation is only effective if traffic is actually redirected to peer resources; the steering mechanism and its propagation latency are critical to meeting the sub-60-second SLA.

generated_requirements.text: Traffic steering changes triggered by peering activation or deactivation must not violate the CDN operator's upstream transit agreements or Regional Internet Registry (RIR) resource allocation policies; the system must validate proposed routing changes against a configured set of transit policy constraints before applying them.
generated_requirements.rationale: Automated BGP or DNS changes that violate transit agreements or RIR policies could result in route hijacking, blackholing, or contractual breach.

generated_requirements.text: The system must incorporate a traffic demand model that accounts for both anticipated traffic patterns (based on historical data and scheduled events) and unanticipated traffic patterns (modeled statistically, e.g., flash crowds); resource acquisition decisions must be evaluated against this combined demand model.
generated_requirements.rationale: BR-7 prohibits capacity planning based solely on historical averages; both anticipated and unanticipated demand must be modeled to prevent reactive-only resource management.

generated_requirements.text: All data exchanged between CDN peers during negotiation that contains personal data attributable to EU data subjects must be minimized to only what is strictly necessary for the negotiation purpose; the system must not transmit CDN topology details, customer metadata, or content provider information beyond what is required, in compliance with GDPR Articles 5, 28, and 44–49.
generated_requirements.rationale: RC-1 and GDPR data minimization obligations require that inter-CDN negotiation messages not expose unnecessary personal or customer data to peer CDN operators.

generated_requirements.text: When PA↔PA policies are updated while a peering arrangement is in progress, the system must apply a defined consistency model — either eventual or strong consistency — and must handle in-flight negotiations that straddle a policy change boundary by either completing under the prior policy version or safely aborting and restarting under the new policy version, with the chosen behavior configurable per policy type.
generated_requirements.rationale: Mid-arrangement policy updates create a consistency boundary that must be explicitly managed to prevent in-flight negotiations from being evaluated against an inconsistent policy state.

generated_requirements.text: All inter-component interaction protocols must be designed to operate over existing public internet infrastructure without requiring dedicated private interconnects between CDN peers; the protocols must tolerate packet loss, variable latency, and out-of-order delivery characteristic of internet paths.
generated_requirements.rationale: TC-2 requires that protocols function over existing internet infrastructure, meaning they must be resilient to typical internet path characteristics.

generated_requirements.text: All protocols and message formats defined by the system must be vendor-neutral and based on open standards where applicable; proprietary formats or protocols that would prevent interoperability with compliant CDN operators from different vendors are not permitted.
generated_requirements.rationale: TC-1 requires vendor-neutral, open-standards-based protocols to enable interoperability across heterogeneous CDN environments.

generated_requirements.text: The system must achieve an SLA satisfaction rate of at least 99.5% across all active peering agreements, measured as the percentage of SLA measurement periods in which all defined SLA metrics are within their specified thresholds.
generated_requirements.rationale: BO-4 sets a hard KPI of ≥ 99.5% SLA satisfaction rate across all active peering agreements.

generated_requirements.text: The system must require that SLA violation consequence policies be formally defined and deployed before any peering arrangement is activated; the system must block activation of any peering arrangement for which no SLA violation consequence policy exists.
generated_requirements.rationale: BR-11 prohibits activation of peering arrangements without pre-defined SLA violation consequences.

generated_requirements.text: The system must require that all functional policies governing system behavior be formally identified, approved, and deployed before the system handles live peering traffic; the system must prevent live peering traffic processing if any required functional policy is absent or in a draft state.
generated_requirements.rationale: BR-13 prohibits ad hoc or undocumented operational decisions; all functional policies must be formally deployed before live traffic is handled.

generated_requirements.text: The policy configuration and protocol management interfaces must be operable by technically proficient CDN operations staff without requiring software engineering expertise; the system must provide a structured configuration interface (e.g., form-based or schema-validated configuration files) with inline validation and descriptive error messages.
generated_requirements.rationale: OC-4 requires that the system be operable by non-developer operations staff, necessitating accessible policy and protocol management interfaces.

generated_requirements.text: The system must produce a complete, immutable audit trail for every peering negotiation event, including: negotiation initiation, each message exchanged, policy evaluations performed, resource allocation decisions, SLA measurements, violation detections, consequence applications, renegotiation attempts, and teardown events; each audit record must include a trusted timestamp, component identifier, and correlation identifier linking all records for a single peering arrangement.
generated_requirements.rationale: RC-2 requires auditable records sufficient to support legal or contractual dispute resolution, necessitating a comprehensive, correlated audit trail across the full peering lifecycle.

generated_requirements.text: The system must maintain a peering arrangement success rate such that fewer than 5% of negotiation events require manual intervention, measured across all short-term and long-term negotiation attempts over any rolling 30-day period.
generated_requirements.rationale: BO-1 sets a KPI that manual intervention is required in fewer than 5% of negotiation events, reflecting the degree of automation reliability required.

domain: Content Delivery Network (CDN) / Distributed Internet Infrastructure
sme_subject: CDN Peering and Interconnection Architect with expertise in inter-domain routing protocols, SLA engineering, and network policy automation
sme_advisory: This CDN Peering Negotiation and Resource Management System sits at the intersection of inter-domain routing policy, real-time SLA enforcement, and distributed systems security — areas where generalist requirements engineers frequently under-specify. The engineer should pay particular attention to the stateful nature of peering negotiation sessions (including partial-failure and rollback semantics), the trust hierarchy between Policy Agents across administrative domains, and the latency budget decomposition required to meet sub-60-second short-term negotiation SLAs. Requirements must explicitly address protocol versioning and backward compatibility, since heterogeneous CDN environments will inevitably run different software generations simultaneously, and a single incompatible message exchange can silently degrade or block an entire peering workflow.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall provide a publicly accessible, multilingual pre-VCD mapping tool that maps national attestations from each participating Member State to the European qualitative selection and exclusion criteria defined in Articles 45–50 of Directive 2004/18/EC.
generated_requirements.rationale: This is the core Stage 1 deliverable (BO-01, BO-02) and the mandatory prerequisite for all subsequent VCD stages per dependency D-01.

generated_requirements.text: The pre-VCD mapping tool shall enforce that all European criteria represented within it are derived exclusively from Articles 45–50 of Directive 2004/18/EC and Annex X; the system shall reject any attempt to introduce criteria inconsistent with the Directive.
generated_requirements.rationale: BR-01 and C-01 establish this as a hard legal constraint; violation renders procurement procedures legally challengeable.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall enforce equal treatment of foreign and domestic economic operators by ensuring that, for any given European criterion, the evidence burden presented to a foreign economic operator does not exceed that presented to a domestic economic operator in the same Member State, in compliance with Article 52(5) of Directive 2004/18/EC.
generated_requirements.rationale: BO-06 and the domain expert advisory identify Article 52(5) equal-treatment enforcement as a legally critical system behaviour.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall treat EFTA EEA jurisdictions (Norway, Iceland, Liechtenstein) as legally equivalent to EU Member States for all mapping, evidence exchange, and cross-border authorisation operations, in accordance with EFTA EEA Committee Decision 68/2006.
generated_requirements.rationale: C-02 and the domain expert advisory require EEA/EFTA states to be accommodated within the same legal and technical framework as EU Member States.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall produce national mapping instances using a common, agreed mapping template that generates outputs that are both machine-computable and human-readable, with each mapping artefact recording the national attestation, the corresponding European criterion, the issuing body, and any permitted substitute evidences.
generated_requirements.rationale: BR-03 and C-12 require the mapping template to support both automated VCD assembly and manual interpretation by contracting authorities.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall enforce a formal legal approval workflow for all mapping artefacts — including the standard representation of European criteria, the mapping template, and each national mapping instance — requiring sign-off from relevant national and European authorities before any mapping is published or deployed.
generated_requirements.rationale: BR-04, C-08, and D-06 establish legal approval as a hard prerequisite for deployment; absence of this workflow creates legal invalidity risk.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall provide an automated change management workflow that detects relevant legal changes at national or European level, triggers a mapping update task, routes the update through the formal approval process, and publishes the approved update within five (5) business days of the legal change entering into force.
generated_requirements.rationale: BR-05, C-19, and SC-03/SC-04 quantify the five-business-day update obligation as a measurable success criterion.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall enforce that no change to any mapping artefact may be applied without completion of a formally defined change management procedure and recorded agreement from the governing body, preventing unilateral or unapproved modifications.
generated_requirements.rationale: BR-06 establishes change governance as a mandatory control to preserve legal validity and consistency of the mapping framework.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall accommodate, within the mapping framework, substitute evidences — including declarations on oath, solemn declarations, and notarial statements — for Member States that do not issue a particular document or certificate, as permitted by Directive 2004/18/EC.
generated_requirements.rationale: BR-08 requires the system to handle the legal reality that not all Member States issue equivalent documents for every criterion.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall provide identity and access management functionality enabling registration, authentication, and account management for all stakeholder types (economic operators, contracting authorities, national VCD service providers, issuing bodies), with single-sign-on implemented across WP2 and WP8 systems.
generated_requirements.rationale: BR-17, C-15, and SC-11 mandate authenticated access and SSO integration with WP8 as prerequisites for Stage 2 operation.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall enforce that sensitive economic operator data is verified by an authorised officer through a formal procedure before a VCD account is created, and that the officer — not the economic operator — places sensitive data into the account.
generated_requirements.rationale: BR-18 establishes this as a trust and data integrity control to prevent self-certification of sensitive qualification data.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall enforce role-based access controls such that economic operators may only access their own VCD accounts and packages, and contracting authorities may only access VCD packages for which they hold a valid, stored mandate from the relevant economic operator.
generated_requirements.rationale: BR-19 and BR-21 define the access control boundaries that protect economic operator data from unauthorised disclosure.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall provide a formal change procedure for modifying sensitive economic operator data associated with a VCD account, and shall allow economic operators to permanently delete their accounts and all associated personal data.
generated_requirements.rationale: BR-20 and national data protection obligations (C-03) require controlled data modification and the right to erasure.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall allow economic operators to electronically collect, compile, and assemble a VCD simple package containing, at minimum: structural VCD package data, economic operator data, tender-specific data, structural data of evidence, and the evidences themselves, each linked to a unique VCD instance identifier and, where applicable, a specific Call for Tender.
generated_requirements.rationale: BR-09 and BR-11 define the mandatory content and uniqueness requirements for every VCD package instance.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall provide interfaces to national issuing bodies enabling automated retrieval of attestations in electronic form, and shall provide a manual upload mechanism as a fallback for attestations that cannot be retrieved automatically, ensuring a complete VCD package can always be produced.
generated_requirements.rationale: BR-12 and BO-03 require both automated and manual evidence collection paths to handle the heterogeneous maturity of national issuing body systems.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall generate a compilation suggestion for the economic operator that is verified as complete and compliant with the tender-specific requirements before presentation; the system shall not present a compilation suggestion that is incomplete or non-compliant.
generated_requirements.rationale: BR-14 establishes reliability of the compilation suggestion as a mandatory functional requirement to prevent disqualification of valid bids.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall embed within each VCD package metadata describing the security and quality-enhancing policies applied by the certified national VCD service provider during package compilation.
generated_requirements.rationale: BR-13 requires this metadata to enable contracting authorities to assess the trustworthiness of the compilation process.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall transmit VCD packages to contracting authorities via the PEPPOL WP8 infrastructure as the primary transport channel, supporting the economic operator's right to choose the submission channel while strongly preferring PEPPOL transport.
generated_requirements.rationale: BR-23 and C-09 establish PEPPOL WP8 as the primary transport mechanism while preserving the economic operator's channel choice.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall issue a certified timestamp at the moment of VCD package submission and at the moment of confirmed receipt by the contracting authority, storing both timestamps in the audit log and making them available for verification that submission preceded the closure of the relevant Call for Tender.
generated_requirements.rationale: BR-25 and SC-12 identify certified timestamping as legally critical for proving timely submission and preventing disqualification on procedural grounds.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall apply cryptographic integrity protection to every VCD package such that any alteration to the package content after signing is detectable upon receipt, and shall provide non-repudiation of origin through advanced or qualified electronic signatures compliant with Directive 1999/93/EC and applicable national signature requirements.
generated_requirements.rationale: BR-24, SC-06, and the domain expert advisory require non-repudiation and integrity verification as legally admissible guarantees, not merely transport-layer security.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall integrate with the PEPPOL WP1 validation service to perform electronic signature validation, data integrity verification, and confidentiality assurance on VCD packages, including in scenarios where the PEPPOL WP8 transport infrastructure is not used.
generated_requirements.rationale: BR-26, C-10, and D-04 establish WP1 integration as a mandatory dependency for legal admissibility of VCD packages.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall provide a VCD viewing system for contracting authorities that can display all data formats contained within a single VCD package, including foreign-language attestations, structured evidence data, and context-specific data, within a single unified interface.
generated_requirements.rationale: C-17 and BO-03 require contracting authorities to be able to interpret the full content of received VCD packages without external tools.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall enforce logical and technical separation between the VCD qualification package and the tender bid itself, preventing the two from being conflated in any submission workflow, in compliance with national transpositions of Directive 2004/18/EC.
generated_requirements.rationale: A-12 and the domain expert advisory identify conflation of VCD and bid as a legal invalidity risk for the procurement procedure.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall implement a stage-gated authorisation workflow whereby a national VCD solution must be formally authorised by the European VCD Service Provider — including successful re-processing of historical tenders — before it may participate in cross-border operations, with re-authorisation automatically triggered by any software update to the national solution.
generated_requirements.rationale: BR-29, SC-07, and C-20 establish cross-border authorisation as a governance workflow requirement with re-authorisation on every update.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall collect and process context-specific data — structured, machine-interpretable representations of evidence content — for each evidence type, following a common standard schema agreed by all pilot partners, with semantic definitions for every schema element formulated in accordance with ISO/IEC 11179.
generated_requirements.rationale: BR-15, C-13, and C-16 define the Stage 3 context-specific data requirement and its ISO/IEC 11179 metadata compliance obligation.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall present context-specific data to contracting authorities as supplementary information alongside the original attestation, and shall not automatically substitute context-specific data for a certified translation; the decision to accept context-specific data in lieu of a certified translation shall remain with the contracting authority.
generated_requirements.rationale: BR-16 reserves the substitution decision to the contracting authority to preserve legal validity and avoid imposing unilateral interpretation.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall provide VCD merging functionality enabling multiple VCD packages from different economic operators (e.g. consortium members) to be combined into a single consolidated VCD package for joint submission.
generated_requirements.rationale: Stage 3 scope and BO-04 require consortium support as a key use case for the advanced VCD package.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall provide VCD recompilation functionality that, when an existing VCD package is reused for a new tender, automatically identifies and flags evidence items that have expired, been superseded by legal changes, or are no longer mapped to the new tender's criteria, and prompts the economic operator to replace them before submission.
generated_requirements.rationale: The domain expert advisory identifies recompilation logic as a complex business rule critical to preventing submission of stale or non-compliant evidence.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall provide mandate management functionality enabling economic operators to create, amend, time-bound, suspend, and revoke mandates authorising specific contracting authorities to retrieve attestations on demand, with every state change logged and timestamped in the audit trail.
generated_requirements.rationale: BR-21 and the domain expert advisory require full mandate lifecycle management as a Stage 4 access control and legal accountability mechanism.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall enable contracting authorities, holding a valid economic operator mandate, to retrieve attestations on demand directly from national VCD service providers, and shall verify that each retrieved attestation is still valid at the time of retrieval relative to the award date or Call for Tender closure date.
generated_requirements.rationale: BR-21, BR-22, and BO-05 define on-demand attestation retrieval and validity checking as the core Stage 4 functional capability.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall implement a conflict resolution workflow for Stage 4 that handles failed on-demand attestation or translation delivery, including defined escalation paths, SLA breach notification to all affected parties, and a fallback mechanism allowing the economic operator to submit the attestation manually.
generated_requirements.rationale: BR-32 and the domain expert advisory identify the absence of a conflict resolution workflow as leaving contracting authorities with no recourse on delivery failure.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall maintain a VCD index containing a list of recognised public authorities able to issue specific attestations in each participating Member State, and shall keep this index current as issuing bodies change.
generated_requirements.rationale: BR-37 requires an authoritative, maintained registry of issuing bodies to support accurate mapping and automated attestation retrieval.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall accommodate jurisdiction-specific legal constraints on the handling of sensitive attestation types — in particular criminal record extracts under Article 45(1) — by supporting direct transmission of such documents to the requesting contracting authority rather than routing them through the economic operator, where national law requires this.
generated_requirements.rationale: BR-34, C-05, and the domain expert advisory identify criminal record extracts as a legally sensitive evidence type requiring special routing logic.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall support the discovery of verified electronic addresses for all identified parties (economic operators, contracting authorities, national VCD service providers) using the PEPPOL WP8 addressing and discovery services, and shall use a jointly agreed identifier allocation strategy for all WP2 stakeholders.
generated_requirements.rationale: Stage 4 scope, D-10, and C-09 require PEPPOL-compatible addressing and discovery as prerequisites for networked package operation.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall maintain a complete, immutable audit trail of all VCD creation, compilation, submission, retrieval, mandate state change, and mapping update events, with each event record carrying a certified timestamp and the unique identifier of the acting party.
generated_requirements.rationale: SC-12 mandates 100% audit trail completeness with certified timestamps as a measurable success criterion for legal accountability.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall enforce that all data elements defined in the VCD schema — including context-specific data schema elements — are formulated in accordance with ISO/IEC 11179, with each element having a formally registered name, definition, representation, and set of permissible values.
generated_requirements.rationale: C-16 mandates ISO/IEC 11179 compliance for all VCD data element definitions to ensure semantic interoperability across Member States.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall support multiple data formats from multiple national issuing body sources and compile them into a single, structurally valid VCD package conforming to the agreed VCD package schema.
generated_requirements.rationale: C-14 requires multi-format ingestion capability to accommodate the heterogeneous output formats of national issuing bodies across Member States.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall maintain all structured data elements, schema labels, user interface strings, and multilingual metadata assets in all official languages of the European Union, with version-controlled translation assets updated in synchronisation with schema changes.
generated_requirements.rationale: SC-09, C-18, and the domain expert advisory require multilingual coverage at the data and schema level, not merely the interface skin.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall be accessible to economic operators of any organisational size — including SMEs — requiring only internet access and a valid electronic identity, with no technical or administrative barrier that would disproportionately disadvantage smaller organisations.
generated_requirements.rationale: SC-08, C-22, and BO-06 establish SME accessibility as a core design principle to fulfil the single market objectives of Directive 2004/18/EC.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall enforce that a national VCD solution may only be authorised for cross-border operation after the legal status, responsibilities, and liability of the relevant national VCD service provider have been formally established in national law, and that the European VCD Service Provider's legal status is similarly established before pan-European governance functions are exercised.
generated_requirements.rationale: BR-27, C-07, D-05, and the domain expert advisory identify legal establishment of service providers as a hard prerequisite for legal admissibility of compiled VCD packages.

generated_requirements.text: The pre-VCD mapping tool shall achieve a minimum availability of 99% during business hours across all participating Member States, with planned maintenance windows scheduled outside business hours and communicated in advance to all stakeholders.
generated_requirements.rationale: SC-02 explicitly quantifies the 99% uptime target for the mapping tool as a measurable success criterion.

generated_requirements.text: The VCD transport infrastructure shall achieve zero loss of VCD packages in transit, with end-to-end delivery confirmation and automatic retry mechanisms ensuring that every submitted package is either successfully delivered or the submitting party is notified of failure within a defined SLA window.
generated_requirements.rationale: SC-05 mandates zero package loss as a measurable reliability target, reflecting the legal consequences of non-delivery in procurement procedures.

generated_requirements.text: All VCD package transport shall be protected by confidentiality controls — including encryption in transit and at rest — ensuring that the content of qualification documents is not accessible to any party other than the authorised economic operator, the mandated contracting authority, and the national VCD service provider, in compliance with national data protection legislation.
generated_requirements.rationale: BR-24, C-03, and BR-33 require confidentiality of sensitive economic operator data as a legal compliance obligation across all participating jurisdictions.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall enforce that 100% of transported VCD packages are verifiably unaltered upon receipt, with cryptographic integrity verification (e.g. digital signature or hash verification) performed automatically on receipt and the result recorded in the audit trail.
generated_requirements.rationale: SC-06 quantifies 100% integrity verification as a measurable success criterion, and BR-24 mandates verifiable integrity as a transport security requirement.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall accommodate varying national electronic signature requirements — including advanced and qualified electronic signatures based on qualified certificates and secure signature creation devices — without rejecting valid foreign signatures that satisfy the requirements of Directive 1999/93/EC, ensuring cross-border signature interoperability.
generated_requirements.rationale: C-04, BR-36, and the domain expert advisory require the system to handle divergent national signature mandates without discriminating against valid foreign signatures.

generated_requirements.text: The pre-VCD mapping tool user interface shall be available in all official languages of the European Union, with language selection persisted across sessions and all UI strings, help texts, and error messages fully translated and maintained in each language.
generated_requirements.rationale: SC-09 and C-18 explicitly require full multilingual UI coverage as a measurable success criterion for the mapping tool.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall be designed and operated in full compliance with the national data protection legislation of each participating Member State, interpreted in light of Directive 95/46/EC (and GDPR Regulation 2016/679), with a documented data protection impact assessment completed before cross-border data exchange is activated for each Member State.
generated_requirements.rationale: C-03 and BR-33 mandate data protection compliance as a legal constraint, with criminal record extracts and personal attestation data being particularly sensitive categories.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall be maintainable such that national mapping instances can be updated, approved, and published by authorised national administrators without requiring changes to the core system software, and such that new Member State instances can be onboarded without architectural changes.
generated_requirements.rationale: The five-business-day update obligation (BR-05, C-19) and the multi-Member-State scalability requirement demand a maintainable, configuration-driven mapping management architecture.

generated_requirements.text: The VCD system architecture shall be interoperable with the PEPPOL WP8 infrastructure's addressing, discovery, and access point mechanisms, and with the WP1 validation service API, such that integration points are defined by published, versioned interface specifications and changes to WP8 or WP1 interfaces trigger a formal impact assessment and update cycle for WP2.
generated_requirements.rationale: C-09, C-10, D-03, and D-04 establish WP8 and WP1 interoperability as hard technical constraints on which the entire VCD transport and validation capability depends.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall be scalable to support simultaneous access by economic operators, contracting authorities, and national VCD service providers across all participating Member States without degradation of response times during peak procurement periods, with performance benchmarks established and validated during pilot testing.
generated_requirements.rationale: SC-08 and C-22 require the system to be accessible to all organisations regardless of size, implying the infrastructure must handle concurrent multi-jurisdictional load without exclusionary performance degradation.

sme_subject: EU public procurement legal and compliance expert specialising in Directive 2004/18/EC and cross-border electronic evidence exchange
sme_advisory: The PEPPOL WP2 VCD solution operates at the intersection of EU public procurement law, cross-border electronic evidence exchange, and pan-European digital infrastructure governance. Requirements engineers must pay particular attention to the layered legal hierarchy — Directive 2004/18/EC Articles 45–52 sit above national transpositions, and any system behaviour that conflicts with either layer creates legal invalidity risk for the entire procurement procedure. The four-stage maturity model means requirements must be elicited with explicit stage-gating conditions, and non-functional requirements around legal admissibility, certified timestamping, non-repudiation, and data sovereignty are as critical as functional ones — overlooking them risks producing a technically functional system that is legally inadmissible in one or more Member States.

ground_truth_requirements.text: A VCD will facilitate electronic Tendering by providing cross-border data and document solution that contains the necessary attestations and certificates typically required in eTendering. Thus it can be seen as a container for documents. Yet up to now, the tendering documents such as attestations and certificates required in public procurement procedures differ between Member States. When a contracting authority publishes a contract notice, it shall include the selection, qualification and nonexclusion criteria. The economic operators have to submit evidence and proof in respect of these criteria. Hence, during the preparation of the tender an economic operator needs to collect the respective evidences (i.e. certificates and qualification documents) from a number of issuing bodies (e.g. public registries, banks, pre-qualification bodies, etc.) to prove conformance with the given selection and exclusion criteria. The overall aims and expectations of a virtual company dossier solution as set out in the description of work can be described as follows: A VCD will facilitate electronic Tendering by providing cross-border data and document solution that contains the necessary attestations and certificates typically required in eTendering. Thus it can be seen as a container for documents. Yet up to now, the tendering documents such as attestations and certificates required in public procurement procedures differ between Member States. When a contracting authority publishes a contract notice, it shall include the selection, qualification and nonexclusion criteria. The economic operators have to submit evidence and proof in respect of these criteria. Hence, during the preparation of the tender an economic operator needs to collect the respective evidences (i.e. certificates and qualification documents) from a number of issuing bodies (e.g. public registries, banks, pre-qualification bodies, etc.) to prove conformance with the given selection and exclusion criteria. The overall aims and expectations of a virtual company dossier solution as set out in the description of work can be described as follows: A major challenge of PEPPOLs’ work package 2 is to integrate various stakeholders in the development process and to set up an IT system which supports a common set of evidences based on electronic business certificates and qualification documents that are most frequently required. The criteria must be consistent with directive 2004/18/EC (EC 30.04.2004): 1. The VCD will support any authorised entities (economic operator, intermediary, contracting authority or IT service such as an eTendering system) in creating an electronic information package consisting of the required documentation, evidences, proof, attestations, certificates, declarations and metadata. 2. In order to create the VCD, an implemented IT system will have to collect certificates and attestations from existing registries. It also enables the economic operator to add self-declarations or other documents of formal qualification. 3. Furthermore, the VCD solution supports economic operators in producing a VCD and in enabling them to submit the required documentation (assembled as an information package) to any contracting authority in Europe. 4. In the same way the VCD as an implemented IT system will enable contracting authorities or their eTendering systems to interpret and accept the documentation submitted by the economic operator. 5. Therefore the contracting authority must either specify the documentation that has to be submitted by economic operator or the criteria of qualitative selection and exclusion that have to be fulfilled by the economic operator. 6. For all parties (economic operator, intermediary, contracting authority, issuing bodies) it will be of high importance that the VCD Service Providers are trustworthy; this implies that the services are precise, up-to-date, available and reliable. Criteria are listed in the Directive and can be established by the contracting authority within the contract notice. A key aspect in the VCD development is to support a common set of criteria for qualitative selection and exclusion derived from the directive and their fulfilment through evidences. Work package 2 will not implement a common set of attestations. Thereby it must be taken into account that some countries do not issue such documents or certificates (e.g. in Norway extracts from the judicial record may be produced in respect of economic operators, but not in respect of private persons, for the purpose of a tender procedure) and this hinders economic operators to evidence certain criteria. In such cases, economic operators can only provide similar evidences that fulfil the same criteria. Where the country in question does not issue such documents or certificates, or where these do not cover all the cases they may be replaced by a declaration on oath, a solemn declaration, a notary or a competent professional or trade body, in the country of origin or in the country whence that person comes. So the link between individual evidences and the respective criteria they may approve for (as listed above) must be precisely indicated and mapped in order to support economic operators in their activity. Besides the mapping of criteria and evidences in a respective country, directive 2004/18/EC indicates also the need for accessibility of “Official lists of approved economic operators and certification by bodies established under public or private law” (cf. Article 52). Article 52 indicates a potential solution which may be addressed in the VCD development too. Article 45 - Personal situation of the candidate or tenderer: e.g. absence of conviction,bankruptcy, fulfilment of payments of social security contributions or taxes, etc. Article 46 - suitability to pursue a professional activity: e.g. certificate of registration from thecommercial register Article 47 - economic and financial standing: e.g. balance sheets Article 48 - technical and/or professional ability of economic operators, e.g. certificates ofsatisfactory execution of past works Article 49 - quality assurance standards, e.g. ISO certificates Article 50 - environmental management standards (Article 51 - Additional documentation and information)
ground_truth_requirements.label: FR

ground_truth_requirements.text: A major precondition for the enforcement of stage 1 is the definition of a standard representation of European criteria for qualitative selection and exclusion. These criteria should be derived directly from 09/11/2009 – version 1.1 (approved by EC) the Articles 45-50 of the Directive 2004/18/EC in order to ensure employment in Pan-European public procurement procedures. The right granularity and consistency of criteria will be important to ensure legitimacy and to retrieve adequate mapping results. Thus the criteria may have to be iteratively redefined according to the results given by specific mappings. A major precondition for the enforcement of stage 1 is the definition of a standard representation of European criteria for qualitative selection and exclusion. These criteria should be derived directly from 09/11/2009 – version 1.1 (approved by EC) the Articles 45-50 of the Directive 2004/18/EC in order to ensure employment in Pan-European public procurement procedures. The right granularity and consistency of criteria will be important to ensure legitimacy and to retrieve adequate mapping results. Thus the criteria may have to be iteratively redefined according to the results given by specific mappings. A standard representation of common criteria of qualitative selection and exclusion MUST be defined according to Articles 45-50 of Directive 2004/18/EC. The right granularity and consistency of criteria MUST be found through testing and iterative redefinition in order to ensure legitimacy and to retrieve adequate mapping results. Input will come from the Articles 45-50 of Directive 2004/18/EC
ground_truth_requirements.label: NFR

ground_truth_requirements.text: National requirements of qualitative selection and exclusion for qualitative selection and exclusion have to adequately represent the national legal conditions. They have to be formulated in a way that they can be mapped to common European criteria for qualitative selection and exclusion mentioned by the Articles 45-50. National requirements of qualitative selection and exclusion for qualitative selection and exclusion have to adequately represent the national legal conditions. They have to be formulated in a way that they can be mapped to common European criteria for qualitative selection and exclusion mentioned by the Articles 45-50. National requirements for qualitative selection and exclusion MUST correspond to the EC criteria given in Article 45-50. National criteria for qualitative selection and exclusion SHOULD have the right granularity and should be consistent among themselves. Input for the current function is taken from the National Public Procurement Acts.
ground_truth_requirements.label: NFR

ground_truth_requirements.text: In a first step, all member states do have to establish a mapping of the criteria defined in the relevant EU directive 2004/18/EC to the national attestations giving evidence to these criteria. To ensure a degree of comparability and reliability among the results, the mapping should be based on a common template. For the overall activity it will be important to have the appropriate legal support in order to ensure legitimacy of mappings. In a first step, all member states do have to establish a mapping of the criteria defined in the relevant EU directive 2004/18/EC to the national attestations giving evidence to these criteria. To ensure a degree of comparability and reliability among the results, the mapping should be based on a common template. For the overall activity it will be important to have the appropriate legal support in order to ensure legitimacy of mappings. A template that ensures comparable and reliable mappings between criteria for qualitative selection and exclusion and national attestations MUST be defined. The members of this work package MUST perform the mapping between national attestations and European criteria of qualitative selection and exclusion. The resulting template has to be computable by machines and readable by humans allowing the assembly of VCD packages through manual and/or automated operations.
ground_truth_requirements.label: NFR

ground_truth_requirements.text: In order to achieve trust and an agreement on the mutual recognition of qualification documents as demanded in the directive, all artefacts and mapping results that relate to the mapping of evidence have to be approved from a legal point of view. Compliances to national and European conditions must be ensured in particular for the definition of a standard representation of common criteria of qualitative selection and exclusion, the definition of a mapping template and the mapping between national attestations and the common criteria for qualitative selection and exclusion. In order to achieve trust and an agreement on the mutual recognition of qualification documents as demanded in the directive, all artefacts and mapping results that relate to the mapping of evidence have to be approved from a legal point of view. Compliances to national and European conditions must be ensured in particular for the definition of a standard representation of common criteria of qualitative selection and exclusion, the definition of a mapping template and the mapping between national attestations and the common criteria for qualitative selection and exclusion. Legitimacy of standard representation of common criteria of qualitative selection and exclusion MUST be ensured. Legitimacy of standard mapping template MUST be ensured. Legitimacy of mapping between national attestations and common criteria for qualitative selection and exclusion MUST be ensured.
ground_truth_requirements.label: NFR

ground_truth_requirements.text: The commonly approved national mappings have to be made accessible to the different stakeholders as especially economic operators, contracting authorities and national VCD service providers. For that purpose, a basic infrastructure or system architecture or a mapping facility has to be defined. The mapping facility, independent of how such as “system” is actually implemented, has to provide the services with regard to the pre-VCD mapping tool The commonly approved national mappings have to be made accessible to the different stakeholders as especially economic operators, contracting authorities and national VCD service providers. For that purpose, a basic infrastructure or system architecture or a mapping facility has to be defined. The mapping facility, independent of how such as “system” is actually implemented, has to provide the services with regard to the pre-VCD mapping tool It MUST be defined by which technical means the mapping service will be provided. Centralized or decentralized infrastructure? Will there be one centralized system on a European level hosting and providing the mapping or a decentralized system consisting of interconnected national systems? In the latter case, one could define that each national system only hosts the national mapping or that each one hosts all the different national mappings which would make a synchronization of the different systems necessary. A decision to deploy either a centralised or a decentralized infrastructure that provides the mapping service MUST be made. By which technical means will the mapping be provided? The technical means to provide the mapping service has to be defined in dependence on the choice of the pilot solution. The simplest form without any technical support could for example consist in the one-time distribution of mapping tables in paper-based form. Another solution could consist of a web-based access to a system providing the mapping by querying an underlying database which contains the mapping table. A more advanced solution would be an automated processing of the mapping according to the relevant criteria that therefore have to be specified by the contracting authority in a dedicated electronic format. Also the higher maturity levels defined in this vision (stage 2-4) can use the mapping service in order to retrieve the mapping information. An entity that hosts the mapping facility MUST be defined in dependence of the type of infrastructure and the dedicated governing model. Which entity will host the mapping facility? In dependence of the choice of infrastructures (centralised – decentralized) and the organisation that governs the infrastructure and mapping facility it has to be defined who will be responsible to host the mapping facility.
ground_truth_requirements.label: NFR

ground_truth_requirements.text: A governing model has to be defined in order to deploy the infrastructure, to maintain its state and sustainability over time. In this respect the composition, structure and scope of the governing model has to be formally defined to ensure fulfilment of functions like liability, sustainability and maintainability. The dedicated governing model should have the necessary status and power to govern its assigned functions. A governing model with the necessary status and power MUST be formally defined in order to ensure fulfilment of functions like liability, sustainability and maintainability. A governing model has to be defined in order to deploy the infrastructure, to maintain its state and sustainability over time. In this respect the composition, structure and scope of the governing model has to be formally defined to ensure fulfilment of functions like liability, sustainability and maintainability. The dedicated governing model should have the necessary status and power to govern its assigned functions. A governing model with the necessary status and power MUST be formally defined in order to ensure fulfilment of functions like liability, sustainability and maintainability. Liability: In law a legal liability is a situation in which a person or organisation is financially and legally responsible. The liability of the organisation that governs infrastructure and mapping facility has to be defined in order to ensure trust into the system and particular mapping results? The Liability of organisation that governs the infrastructure and mapping facility MUST be defined Sustainability: Sustainability is the capacity to maintain a certain process or state into the future. The governing model must ensure that the state of the mapping system will be kept over time. Therefore the mapping system has to be based on the commonly agreed mapping and no changes are allowed without a common agreement. The mapping facility MUST ensure sustainability of the system over time based on commonly agreed mapping schemes. Maintainability: The mapping has to be easily maintainable which could be of interest in the case of changes of the legal basis on national or European level. A formal procedure has to be found how changes are applied to the system. Moreover an organisation or institution that governs the process of change management has to be defined. A formal procedure to make changes to the system MUST be defined.
ground_truth_requirements.label: NFR

ground_truth_requirements.text: Stage 1 is the key concept for ensuring interoperability between the different national systems of evidence provision in public procurement procedures. Every higher stage will be depended on the mapping model defined in this stage. Thus this stage will ensure the integration of different stages and interoperability as such. Therefore each national service provider that provides a VCD (stage 2-4) and all relevant key other stakeholder such as economic operators, contracting authorities and certifiers must be able to retrieve the latest mapping information from the mapping facility. The mapping facility MUST be accessible to all relevant key stakeholders such as economic operators, contracting authorities, certifiers and national service providers. Stage 1 is the key concept for ensuring interoperability between the different national systems of evidence provision in public procurement procedures. Every higher stage will be depended on the mapping model defined in this stage. Thus this stage will ensure the integration of different stages and interoperability as such. Therefore each national service provider that provides a VCD (stage 2-4) and all relevant key other stakeholder such as economic operators, contracting authorities and certifiers must be able to retrieve the latest mapping information from the mapping facility. The mapping facility MUST be accessible to all relevant key stakeholders such as economic operators, contracting authorities, certifiers and national service providers.
ground_truth_requirements.label: NFR

ground_truth_requirements.text: Attention has to be paid to a number of requirements such as reliability, accessibility, usability, maintenance, authenticity, actuality, compliance of mapping to laws and national structures, etc. The pre-VCD mapping tool has to be accredited by the European Union and the Member States. A number of functionalities of the mapping tool are: Attention has to be paid to a number of requirements such as reliability, accessibility, usability, maintenance, authenticity, actuality, compliance of mapping to laws and national structures, etc. The pre-VCD mapping tool has to be accredited by the European Union and the Member States. A number of functionalities of the mapping tool are: Besides such functionalities of the tool, some general requirements regarding the operation of the pre- VCD mapping tool have to be implemented: Provision of an update mechanism maintained by the individual Member State. The pre-VCD mapping tool is openly accessible for economic operators and contracting authorities etc. The tool interface is available in the official languages of the European Union. A powerful search mechanism is implemented to deliver good results when economic operators query the pre-VCD mapping tool in order to understand what evidences he can deliver to confer to the selection and exclusion criteria set in a public tender in another Member State. The results are delivered in a format simple to use for the economic operators; e.g. a check list of evidences he can collect from respective issuing bodies in his country, which fulfil the selection criteria and prove the non-conformance with exclusion criteria set by the contracting authority from a different Member State. The Member States shall agree on a common representation format for the information they intend to supply into the knowledge base supporting the tool’s operations. The Member States shall supply the information generated from the analysis of the national context they represent. The Member States need to update the mapping in case of any change within a short time frame (e.g. five days after a legal change has been put into force). The European service provider for the pre-VCD mapping tool has to ensure that European legal changes are implemented quickly (e.g. within five days after enforcement). The European service provider takes responsibility of the operative implementation and compliance of the mapping model implemented in the pre-VCD mapping tool with the respective European and national conditions. Hence, it must be established and funded with clearly defined responsibilities for maintenance and conformance assessment between national solutions. The European service provider sets up a formal definition of the tool generated results. The European service provider defines the criteria for a “service contract” to be formally agreed with “users”.
ground_truth_requirements.label: NFR

ground_truth_requirements.text: The pre-VCD mapping is the prerequisite for the VCD packages described in the subsequent stages. It requires that the European criteria and national criteria have to be elaborated and agreed upon by the different Member States. The major advantage and benefits of stage 1 are as follows: The pre-VCD mapping is the prerequisite for the VCD packages described in the subsequent stages. It requires that the European criteria and national criteria have to be elaborated and agreed upon by the different Member States. The major advantage and benefits of stage 1 are as follows: The major benefit for economic operators lies in the fact that an automatic mapping of criteria to national evidences is provided through a pre-VCD mapping tool, thus omitting the need for a manual and error-prone selection of appropriate evidences for a particular Call for Tender. The mapping guarantees that the submitted attestations are mutually recognized if they correspond to the mapping information given by the system. The mutual recognition implies a formal commitment by contracting authorities that take part of the service chain, or alternatively by a legal enforcement operated by qualified entities. The formal commitment must in turn specify what is taken as pure guidance or qualified template. The contracting authority can as well use the mapping in order to check if the attestations handed in by an economic operator of another country are sufficiently evidencing those selection and exclusion criteria defined for the tender. Through the pre-VCD mapping model, it will also be possible to identify the major issuing bodies of particular evidence. Nevertheless this function will not be available for all kinds of attestation since there may be too many issuing bodies that provide attestations of the same type (e.g. for criteria to prove the economic standing of a candidate or economic operator). Through the pre-VCD mapping tool, both the economic operator and the contracting authority save time and money because they need not to find out by themselves how the attestations and statements existing in the economic operator’s country can fulfill the criteria demanded by the procuring agency of another country. how the attestations and statements provided by the economic operators of different countries fulfill the criteria demanded in the contracting authority’s tender. Through the pre-VCD mapping and pre-VCD mapping tool, higher transparency and better information quality is provided for both of the actors, economic operator and economic operator). Overall, the pre-VCD mapping tool supports in the proof of compliance of evidences of one country to the requested selection and exclusion criteria of another country via a mapping to the European exclusion and selection criteria.
ground_truth_requirements.label: FR

ground_truth_requirements.text: Feature: Application for VCD account This feature describes the necessary functionalities and interfaces that are needed to apply for a VCD account. Requirements: Application for VCD account Feature: Application for VCD account This feature describes the necessary functionalities and interfaces that are needed to apply for a VCD account. Requirements: Application for VCD account Feature: VCD account management This feature describes the necessary functionalities and interfaces that are needed to manage a VCD account. Requirements: VCD account management Identity and access management MUST provide a functionality to register an economic operator in a formal procedure in order to create a VCD account. Identity and access management MUST provide an interface to submit sensitive economic operator data (for example by uploading attestations that verify sensitive economic operator data) in order to allow the economic operator to apply for the creation of a VCD account. Sensitive economic operator data MUST be verified through a formal procedure by an officer. Quality attributes of Identity and access management An officer has to create a VCD account for the economic operator. Sensitive economic operator data MUST be placed to a VCD account by an officer. Identity and access management MUST provide a functionality that enables the economic operator to receive his log-in data. The authentication within identity and access management SHOULD provide a single-sign on functionality that can be used across other systems. A change procedure MUST be defined in order to modify sensitive economic operator data that is connected to the VCD account. The economic operator MUST be able to delete his account. MUST be clearly defined which economic operator data are sensitive and belong to identity and access management. A single-sign on SHOULD be implemented in order to allow identity and access management across different systems. The identity and access management procedures for creating a VCD account SHOULD be harmonized with the registration procedure of the overall infrastructure provided by WP8.
ground_truth_requirements.label: NFR

ground_truth_requirements.text: Feature: Initialization of VCD Request This feature describes the necessary functionalities and interfaces that are needed to request a VCD instance that is addressing a particular Call for Tender. Requirements: Initialization of VCD Request Feature: Initialization of VCD Request This feature describes the necessary functionalities and interfaces that are needed to request a VCD instance that is addressing a particular Call for Tender. Requirements: Initialization of VCD Request Feature: Arrangement of VCD instance This feature describes the necessary functionalities and interfaces that are needed to arrange a VCD instance according to the needs of an economic operator. Requirements: Arrangement of VCD instance The VCD System SHOULD make use of a single-sign-on interface and function in order to submit login data.
